quiz.text: What is the main advantage of Plotly over static Matplotlib images?
options: It produces interactive charts you can zoom, pan, and hover over
It is the only library that can read CSV files
It trains machine learning models directly
quiz.correct_answer: It produces interactive charts you can zoom, pan, and hover over
quiz.explanation: Plotly renders charts as interactive HTML/JS with built-in zoom, pan, and hover tooltips.

quiz.text: In Plotly Express, how do you typically pass data to a chart function like `px.line`?
options: As a DataFrame plus column names as strings (e.g. x="day", y="sales")
As a list of matplotlib axes
quiz.correct_answer: As a DataFrame plus column names as strings (e.g. x="day", y="sales")
quiz.explanation: Plotly Express is DataFrame-native: you hand it a DataFrame and reference columns by name.

quiz.text: To save a Plotly figure as a standalone interactive file you call fig.______("chart.html").
quiz.explanation: `fig.write_html(path)` exports a self-contained interactive HTML file viewable in any browser.

lessons.title: Jupyter Notebook
lessons.description: Use Jupyter Notebook's cell-based, kernel-backed environment for interactive data exploration and reproducible analysis.
lessons.content_markdown:
Jupyter Notebook is the interactive environment where the vast majority of data science and ML prototyping happens. A notebook is a document made of **cells** that you run one at a time. **Code cells** execute Python and display their output — including rendered DataFrames and charts — directly beneath them, while **Markdown cells** hold formatted prose, headings, and math so your analysis reads like a narrative. Behind the notebook runs a **kernel**: a persistent Python process that holds all your variables in memory between cell executions.

## Key ideas
- **Cells run independently but share state**: a variable defined in cell 1 is available in cell 5 because the kernel keeps memory alive.
- **Execution order is not top-to-bottom by default**: the bracket number `[3]` shows the *order you ran* cells, which is a common source of confusion. Use **Kernel > Restart & Run All** to guarantee a clean, reproducible top-to-bottom run.
- **Rich display**: the last expression in a cell is auto-displayed; DataFrames render as HTML tables and Matplotlib/Plotly figures render inline.
- **Magic commands**: `%timeit` benchmarks a line, `%matplotlib inline` embeds plots, and `!pip install ...` runs shell commands.
- **Formats**: notebooks are `.ipynb` (JSON); tools like JupyterLab, VS Code, and Google Colab all open them.

```python
# Cell 1 — define state
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")

# Cell 2 — reuse it; df still lives in the kernel
df.head()   # last expression auto-renders as a table
```

```python
# Magics: benchmark and install inline
%timeit sum(range(1000))
!pip install seaborn
```

## Why it matters for AI
The entire exploratory workflow of an AI engineer — load data, inspect it, plot it, try a transformation, train a quick model, look at the metrics — is a tight feedback loop that notebooks make instantaneous. You keep an expensive-to-load dataset or model in memory and iterate on analysis cells without re-running everything. Google Colab gives you free GPU-backed notebooks for training. The key discipline to learn early: because out-of-order execution can create results you cannot reproduce, always confirm your final analysis with **Restart & Run All** before trusting or sharing it.

examples.title: Launch Jupyter
examples.code:
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate        # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
pip install jupyterlab pandas matplotlib

# Start the notebook server (opens in your browser)
jupyter lab

examples.title: A reproducible analysis cell
examples.code:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Kernel keeps df in memory across cells
df = pd.DataFrame({"month": [1, 2, 3], "sales": [100, 130, 90]})

# The final expression renders inline as a chart
ax = df.plot(x="month", y="sales", marker="o", title="Sales")
plt.show()

quiz.text: Why can two cells in a Jupyter Notebook share variables even though you run them separately?
options: Notebooks save all variables to disk after each cell
A persistent kernel process keeps variables in memory between executions
Each cell re-runs every earlier cell automatically
Variables are stored in the browser's cookies
quiz.correct_answer: A persistent kernel process keeps variables in memory between executions
quiz.explanation: The kernel is a long-lived Python process that retains state, so later cells see earlier variables.

quiz.text: What is the safest way to guarantee your notebook produces the same results from a clean state?
quiz.explanation: Restarting clears memory and running all cells top-to-bottom verifies the analysis is reproducible in order.

quiz.text: In Jupyter, special commands prefixed with % (like %timeit) are called ______ commands.
quiz.explanation: Magic commands (line magics with %, cell magics with %%) provide shortcuts like timing and inline plotting.

title: Machine Learning
description:
This phase takes you from raw data to working predictive models using scikit-learn, the workhorse library of applied machine learning. You will learn the two great families of supervised learning — regression (predicting numbers) and classification (predicting categories) — plus unsupervised clustering, and you will meet the core model architectures every practitioner reaches for: decision trees, random forests, support vector machines, and k-nearest neighbors. Crucially, you will not just call `.fit()` blindly: each lesson unpacks the math (loss functions, impurity, margins, distances) so you understand *why* a model behaves the way it does. You will also master the discipline that separates real engineers from tutorial-followers — honest evaluation through cross-validation and the right metrics — so you never fool yourself with a model that only looks good. These classical methods remain the correct default for tabular data and form the conceptual bedrock beneath deep learning.
Projects to build: House Price Prediction, Spam Detection, Customer Churn, Loan Approval.
Resources: scikit-learn user guide; Hands-On Machine Learning.

lessons.title: Regression
lessons.description: Predicting a continuous numeric target by fitting a function that minimizes prediction error.
lessons.content_markdown:
Regression is supervised learning where the target is a continuous number — a house price, a temperature, a person's blood pressure. The simplest and most important model is **linear regression**, which assumes the target is a weighted sum of the features plus a bias: `ŷ = w₁x₁ + w₂x₂ + ... + b`. Training means choosing the weights `w` and bias `b` that make predictions closest to the truth.

"Closest" is defined by a **loss function**, almost always Mean Squared Error (MSE): the average of the squared differences between predictions and actual values. Squaring punishes big misses heavily and makes the loss a smooth, bowl-shaped (convex) function with a single global minimum. That minimum has a famous closed-form solution, the **Normal Equation** `w = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy`, and can also be found iteratively with **gradient descent** for large datasets.

## Key ideas
- **Coefficients are interpretable**: `w₁ = 500` means each unit of feature 1 adds 500 to the prediction, holding others fixed.
- **MSE vs MAE**: MSE (squared error) is sensitive to outliers; Mean Absolute Error is more robust but non-smooth at zero.
- **Regularization** fights overfitting by penalizing large weights: **Ridge** (L2) shrinks weights, **Lasso** (L1) can drive some to exactly zero, doing feature selection.
- **Non-linearity**: add polynomial features (`x²`, `x₁x₂`) so a linear model can fit curves.
- **R² (coefficient of determination)** reports the fraction of variance the model explains; 1.0 is perfect, 0 is no better than predicting the mean.

```python
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
model = Ridge(alpha=1.0)      # alpha controls regularization strength
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(model.score(X_test, y_test))  # returns R²
```

## Why it matters for AI
Regression is the entry point to all of ML: the linear model's weights, MSE loss, and gradient descent are the *exact* same machinery that powers a neural network — a deep net is essentially stacked regressions with non-linear activations between them. Every forecasting system, recommendation score, and risk model leans on these ideas. Understanding regularization here is what later stops your huge models from memorizing noise.

examples.title: Fit and predict house prices
examples.code:
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import numpy as np

# feature: square footage; target: price
X = np.array([[1500], [1800], [2400], [3000], [3500]])
y = np.array([300000, 350000, 470000, 600000, 690000])

model = LinearRegression().fit(X, y)
print('slope (price per sqft):', round(model.coef_[0], 2))
print('intercept:', round(model.intercept_, 2))
print('predict 2000 sqft:', round(model.predict([[2000]])[0], 2))
print('R^2 on training data:', round(model.score(X, y), 4))

examples.title: Polynomial regression via a pipeline
examples.code:
from sklearn.preprocessing import PolynomialFeatures
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
import numpy as np

X = np.array([[1], [2], [3], [4], [5]])
y = np.array([1, 4, 9, 16, 25])  # y = x^2

model = make_pipeline(PolynomialFeatures(degree=2), LinearRegression())
model.fit(X, y)
print(model.predict([[6]]))  # ~36.0

quiz.text: Which loss function does ordinary linear regression minimize?
quiz.explanation: Linear regression minimizes the average squared difference between predictions and targets, which is convex and has a closed-form solution.

quiz.text: What is the main effect of Lasso (L1) regularization compared to Ridge (L2)?
options: It can set some weights exactly to zero, performing feature selection
quiz.correct_answer: It can set some weights exactly to zero, performing feature selection
quiz.explanation: L1's absolute-value penalty has corners at zero, so it drives unhelpful coefficients to exactly zero, while L2 only shrinks them toward zero.

quiz.text: The metric that reports the fraction of the target's variance explained by a regression model, where 1.0 is perfect, is called ______.
quiz.explanation: R² (the coefficient of determination) compares the model's residual error to the variance of simply predicting the mean.

challenges.title: Least-squares line fit
challenges.description: Implement simple linear regression from scratch. Given parallel lists of x and y values, return the best-fit slope and intercept using the ordinary least-squares formulas, each rounded to 4 decimals.
challenges.starter_code:
def linear_regression(xs, ys):
    # Return (slope, intercept) for the least-squares line y = slope*x + intercept.
    # slope = sum((x-mx)(y-my)) / sum((x-mx)^2);  intercept = my - slope*mx
    pass
hints: Compute the means mx and my first, then loop once to accumulate the numerator and denominator.
Round each returned value with round(value, 4) before packing them into a tuple.
challenges.solution_code:
def linear_regression(xs, ys):
    n = len(xs)
    mx = sum(xs) / n
    my = sum(ys) / n
    num = sum((xs[i] - mx) * (ys[i] - my) for i in range(n))
    den = sum((xs[i] - mx) ** 2 for i in range(n))
    slope = num / den
    intercept = my - slope * mx
    return (round(slope, 4), round(intercept, 4))

lessons.title: Classification
lessons.description: Predicting a discrete class label by learning a decision boundary between categories.
lessons.content_markdown:
Classification is supervised learning where the target is a **category** rather than a number: spam vs. ham, churn vs. stay, digit 0–9. The model outputs a class label, and usually a probability behind it.

The foundational model is **logistic regression** — which, despite the name, is a classifier. It computes a linear score `z = w·x + b`, then squashes it through the **sigmoid** function `σ(z) = 1 / (1 + e⁻ᶻ)` to get a probability between 0 and 1. If the probability exceeds a threshold (usually 0.5), it predicts the positive class. Training minimizes **cross-entropy (log) loss**, which heavily penalizes confident wrong answers — predicting 0.99 for something that was actually class 0 is punished far more than predicting 0.6.

## Key ideas
- **Decision boundary**: the surface where the model is exactly 50/50. For logistic regression it is linear; other models bend it.
- **Binary vs. multiclass**: extend to many classes with **softmax** (one score per class, normalized to sum to 1) or one-vs-rest.
- **Probabilities, not just labels**: use `predict_proba` to rank, set custom thresholds, or feed downstream systems.
- **Class imbalance**: if 99% of email is ham, always-predict-ham gets 99% accuracy but is useless — handle with `class_weight`, resampling, or better metrics.

```python
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
clf = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(clf.predict_proba(X_test)[:3])   # probability per class
print(clf.predict(X_test)[:3])         # hard labels
```

## Why it matters for AI
Classification is arguably the most common ML task in production — fraud flags, content moderation, medical triage, and the final layer of nearly every deep neural network is a softmax classifier trained with cross-entropy. The sigmoid and cross-entropy loss you learn here are *literally* the output and objective of large classifiers, including the token-prediction head of a language model. Understanding thresholds and imbalance is what keeps a deployed classifier from being confidently, expensively wrong.

examples.title: Binary classification with probabilities
examples.code:
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)
X_tr, X_te, y_tr, y_te = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=0)

clf = LogisticRegression(max_iter=5000).fit(X_tr, y_tr)
print('accuracy:', round(clf.score(X_te, y_te), 4))
print('P(class=1) for first 3:', clf.predict_proba(X_te)[:3, 1].round(3))

examples.title: Custom threshold instead of 0.5
examples.code:
import numpy as np
# probs from predict_proba for the positive class
probs = np.array([0.2, 0.45, 0.55, 0.9])

# Be stricter: only flag as positive above 0.6
preds = (probs >= 0.6).astype(int)
print(preds)  # [0 0 0 1]

quiz.text: What does the sigmoid function output in logistic regression?
options: A value between 0 and 1 interpreted as a probability
quiz.correct_answer: A value between 0 and 1 interpreted as a probability
quiz.explanation: Sigmoid squashes the linear score z into (0, 1), which is read as the probability of the positive class.

quiz.text: The loss function minimized when training logistic regression, which harshly penalizes confident wrong predictions, is called ______ loss.
quiz.explanation: Cross-entropy (log loss) grows sharply as the predicted probability moves away from the true label.

quiz.text: Why can accuracy be misleading on a spam dataset that is 99% ham?
options: Accuracy cannot be computed on text
A model that always predicts 'ham' scores 99% while catching zero spam
quiz.correct_answer: A model that always predicts 'ham' scores 99% while catching zero spam
quiz.explanation: With heavy class imbalance, a trivial majority-class predictor looks excellent by accuracy yet is useless, so precision/recall matter more.

challenges.title: Implement the sigmoid function
challenges.description: Write the logistic sigmoid used to convert a linear score into a probability. Return the result rounded to 4 decimals.
challenges.starter_code:
import math

def sigmoid(z):
    # Return 1 / (1 + e^(-z)), rounded to 4 decimals.
    pass
hints: Use math.exp(-z) for the exponential term.
sigmoid(0) should be exactly 0.5.

lessons.title: Clustering
lessons.description: Grouping unlabeled data into clusters of similar points without any target variable.
lessons.content_markdown:
Clustering is **unsupervised** learning: there are no labels, only features, and the goal is to discover natural groups. Given customers described by spending and frequency, clustering finds segments no one told the algorithm about in advance.

The canonical algorithm is **K-Means**. You choose the number of clusters `k`, and the algorithm alternates two steps until stable: (1) **assign** each point to its nearest cluster center (centroid), using Euclidean distance; (2) **update** each centroid to the mean of the points assigned to it. This iteration provably decreases the **inertia** — the total squared distance from points to their centroids — until it converges to a (local) minimum.

## Key ideas
- **Choosing k**: K-Means can't tell you k. Use the **elbow method** (plot inertia vs. k, look for the bend) or the **silhouette score** (how well-separated clusters are, from -1 to 1).
- **Sensitive to scale**: because it uses distance, always **standardize features** first, or the large-range feature dominates.
- **Assumes round, similar-sized clusters**: it struggles with elongated or nested shapes — use **DBSCAN** (density-based) for those, which also finds outliers and needs no k.
- **Initialization matters**: `k-means++` seeds centroids smartly to avoid poor local minima.

```python
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

Xs = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)   # scale first!
km = KMeans(n_clusters=3, n_init=10, random_state=0).fit(Xs)
print(km.labels_)      # cluster id per point
print(km.inertia_)     # total within-cluster squared distance
```

## Why it matters for AI
Clustering powers customer segmentation, anomaly detection, image compression (color quantization), and document topic grouping. In modern AI it underlies **vector search**: embeddings from a neural network are clustered or indexed by distance to enable retrieval-augmented generation. The core intuition — that meaning lives in *distance* within a feature space — is the same idea that makes embeddings and semantic search work.

examples.title: K-Means with the elbow method
examples.code:
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs

X, _ = make_blobs(n_samples=300, centers=4, random_state=42)

for k in range(1, 7):
    km = KMeans(n_clusters=k, n_init=10, random_state=0).fit(X)
    print(f'k={k}  inertia={km.inertia_:.1f}')
# Look for the k where inertia stops dropping sharply (the 'elbow').

examples.title: Silhouette score to judge cluster quality
examples.code:
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
from sklearn.datasets import make_blobs

X, _ = make_blobs(n_samples=300, centers=4, random_state=42)
km = KMeans(n_clusters=4, n_init=10, random_state=0).fit(X)
print('silhouette:', round(silhouette_score(X, km.labels_), 4))  # closer to 1 is better

quiz.text: What are the two alternating steps of the K-Means algorithm?
options: Assign points to nearest centroid, then move centroids to the mean of their points
quiz.correct_answer: Assign points to nearest centroid, then move centroids to the mean of their points
quiz.explanation: K-Means repeats assignment and centroid-update steps, monotonically reducing inertia until convergence.

quiz.text: Why should you standardize features before running K-Means?
options: Because distance-based clustering lets large-range features dominate otherwise
To reduce the number of clusters
quiz.correct_answer: Because distance-based clustering lets large-range features dominate otherwise
quiz.explanation: K-Means relies on Euclidean distance, so a feature measured in thousands would overwhelm one measured in single digits unless scaled.

quiz.text: The total within-cluster squared distance that K-Means minimizes is called ______.
quiz.explanation: Inertia (within-cluster sum of squares) measures how tight the clusters are; lower is tighter.

challenges.title: Assign a point to the nearest centroid
challenges.description: Implement the assignment step of K-Means. Given a point and a list of centroids, return the index of the centroid with the smallest Euclidean distance to the point.
challenges.starter_code:
import math

def nearest_centroid(point, centroids):
    # Return the index of the centroid closest to point (Euclidean distance).
    pass
hints: Euclidean distance is sqrt(sum of squared differences per dimension).
You can compare squared distances since sqrt is monotonic, or use min with a key.

lessons.title: Decision Trees
lessons.description: A model that predicts by asking a sequence of yes/no questions, splitting data to reduce impurity.
lessons.content_markdown:
A decision tree makes predictions by asking a cascade of simple threshold questions — "Is income > $50k?", "Is age < 30?" — following branches down to a **leaf** that holds the answer. It is the most human-readable model in ML: you can literally print the rules.

Training is a greedy, recursive process. At each node the algorithm searches every feature and every threshold to find the split that best **separates the classes**, measured by a reduction in **impurity**. For classification the impurity is usually **Gini impurity** `1 − Σ pᵢ²` (probability of mislabeling a random sample) or **entropy** `−Σ pᵢ log₂ pᵢ`. A pure node — all one class — has impurity 0. For regression trees the criterion is variance (MSE) reduction instead.

## Key ideas
- **Greedy splitting**: it never backtracks; it picks the locally best split at each node.
- **Overfitting is the danger**: an unconstrained tree grows until every leaf is pure — memorizing noise. Control it with `max_depth`, `min_samples_leaf`, or post-pruning (`ccp_alpha`).
- **No scaling needed**: splits are threshold comparisons, so feature units and monotone transforms don't matter — a big practical convenience.
- **Feature importance**: total impurity reduction attributed to each feature tells you what drove decisions.
- **Handles non-linear, interacting features** naturally, unlike a plain linear model.

```python
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier, export_text
clf = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=0).fit(X, y)
print(export_text(clf, feature_names=list(feature_names)))  # readable rules
```

## Why it matters for AI
A single tree is rarely the final model, but it is the atom of the most powerful classical methods: **random forests** and **gradient-boosted trees** (XGBoost, LightGBM) are ensembles of trees that dominate tabular-data competitions and real business problems. Understanding impurity, greedy splits, and overfitting here is the direct prerequisite for those. Trees are also prized in high-stakes settings (credit, healthcare) precisely because their decisions can be audited and explained.

examples.title: Train a shallow, interpretable tree
examples.code:
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris

X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
clf = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=2, criterion='gini', random_state=0)
clf.fit(X, y)
print('train accuracy:', round(clf.score(X, y), 4))
print('feature importances:', clf.feature_importances_.round(3))

examples.title: How depth controls overfitting
examples.code:
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)
X_tr, X_te, y_tr, y_te = train_test_split(X, y, random_state=0)
for d in [1, 3, 5, None]:
    clf = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=d, random_state=0).fit(X_tr, y_tr)
    print(f'depth={d}  train={clf.score(X_tr,y_tr):.3f}  test={clf.score(X_te,y_te):.3f}')

quiz.text: What does a decision tree try to minimize when choosing a split?
options: The impurity (e.g., Gini or entropy) of the resulting child nodes
quiz.correct_answer: The impurity (e.g., Gini or entropy) of the resulting child nodes
quiz.explanation: Each split is chosen to maximize the drop in impurity, producing children that are more class-pure than the parent.

quiz.text: A node containing samples of only one class has a Gini impurity of ______.
quiz.explanation: With a single class, one probability is 1 and the rest 0, so 1 − Σpᵢ² = 1 − 1 = 0.

quiz.text: Why do decision trees NOT require feature scaling?
options: Splits are threshold comparisons unaffected by monotone rescaling
quiz.correct_answer: Splits are threshold comparisons unaffected by monotone rescaling
quiz.explanation: A split like 'x > t' gives the same partition whether or not x is standardized, so units are irrelevant.

challenges.title: Compute Gini impurity
challenges.description: Given a list of class labels at a node, compute its Gini impurity 1 − Σ pᵢ², rounded to 4 decimals. Labels may be any hashable values.
hints: Count how many times each label appears, then divide by the total to get proportions.
collections.Counter makes tallying the labels easy.

lessons.title: Random Forest
lessons.description: An ensemble of many decorrelated decision trees whose votes are averaged to cut variance.
lessons.content_markdown:
A single deep decision tree is powerful but unstable — small changes in the data can flip its splits, and it overfits. A **random forest** fixes this by training *hundreds* of trees and combining them. For classification it takes a **majority vote**; for regression it **averages** the predictions. Because the errors of independent trees partly cancel, the ensemble has far lower **variance** than any one tree while keeping low bias.

The magic is in making the trees **different from each other** (decorrelated), achieved by two sources of randomness:

## Key ideas
- **Bagging (bootstrap aggregating)**: each tree trains on a random sample of the rows drawn *with replacement*, so every tree sees a slightly different dataset.
- **Feature subsampling**: at each split a tree may only consider a random subset of features (`max_features`), preventing one dominant feature from making all trees look alike.
- **Out-of-bag (OOB) score**: roughly a third of rows are left out of each tree's bootstrap sample and can serve as a free validation set — no separate hold-out needed.
- **Robust defaults**: forests work well with little tuning, resist overfitting as you add trees (more trees never hurts accuracy, only speed), and need no scaling.
- **Feature importance** averaged across trees is more reliable than a single tree's.

```python
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=300, max_features='sqrt',
                            oob_score=True, random_state=0, n_jobs=-1)
rf.fit(X_train, y_train)
print('OOB accuracy:', round(rf.oob_score_, 4))
```

## Why it matters for AI
Random forests are one of the most reliable off-the-shelf models for **tabular data** — the format most business data actually takes — often beating a neural network with a fraction of the effort. They are the go-to baseline for churn, fraud, credit scoring, and ranking. The bias–variance intuition behind them (combine many high-variance learners to get a stable one) is a central theme of all machine learning, and their cousin **gradient boosting** builds on the same tree ensemble idea to reach state-of-the-art tabular performance.

examples.title: Random forest with out-of-bag evaluation
examples.code:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer

X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, oob_score=True,
                            random_state=0, n_jobs=-1).fit(X, y)
print('OOB accuracy:', round(rf.oob_score_, 4))

top = sorted(enumerate(rf.feature_importances_), key=lambda t: -t[1])[:3]
print('top feature indices:', [i for i, _ in top])

examples.title: More trees stabilizes the score
examples.code:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_wine
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

X, y = load_wine(return_X_y=True)
for n in [1, 10, 100, 300]:
    rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=n, random_state=0)
    score = cross_val_score(rf, X, y, cv=5).mean()
    print(f'trees={n:>3}  cv accuracy={score:.3f}')

quiz.text: What is 'bagging' in a random forest?
options: Training each tree on a bootstrap sample of rows drawn with replacement
Combining trees with a neural network
quiz.correct_answer: Training each tree on a bootstrap sample of rows drawn with replacement
quiz.explanation: Bagging gives each tree a different resampled dataset, which decorrelates the trees so their averaged prediction has lower variance.

quiz.text: Besides row sampling, how does a random forest further decorrelate its trees?
options: By considering only a random subset of features at each split
By using a smaller learning rate
quiz.correct_answer: By considering only a random subset of features at each split
quiz.explanation: Feature subsampling stops a single strong feature from making every tree split the same way, increasing diversity.

quiz.text: The roughly one-third of rows left out of each tree's bootstrap sample provide a free validation estimate called the ______ score.
quiz.explanation: Out-of-bag (OOB) samples were never seen by a given tree, so aggregating their predictions estimates generalization without a separate hold-out set.

challenges.title: Majority vote aggregation
challenges.description: Implement the classification step a forest uses to combine tree predictions. Given a list of votes, return the label with the most votes; break ties by returning the smallest label (by normal ordering).
hints: Tally votes with collections.Counter, then find the maximum count.
Among labels that share the top count, use min() to break the tie deterministically.

lessons.title: SVM
lessons.description: A classifier that finds the boundary with the widest possible margin between classes, using kernels for non-linearity.
lessons.content_markdown:
A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** classifies by finding the decision boundary (a hyperplane) that separates the classes with the **largest possible margin** — the widest 'street' between the two groups. Intuitively, the boundary that stays as far as possible from the nearest points of either class will generalize best to new data.

Only the points sitting on the edge of that street matter; they are the **support vectors**, and they alone define the boundary — move any other point and nothing changes. Real data is rarely perfectly separable, so a **soft margin** allows some violations, governed by the hyperparameter **C**: large C means few violations (a hard, low-bias/high-variance fit), small C tolerates more (wider margin, more regularization).

## Key ideas
- **Maximize the margin**: minimize `½‖w‖²` subject to every point being on the correct side, an elegant convex optimization.
- **Hinge loss**: the training objective penalizes a point by `max(0, 1 − y·(w·x + b))` — zero if it's correctly classified beyond the margin, growing linearly as it intrudes.
- **The kernel trick**: replace dot products with a kernel (RBF/Gaussian, polynomial) to draw non-linear boundaries in the original space *without* ever computing high-dimensional coordinates — powerful and efficient.
- **Scaling is mandatory**: SVMs are distance-based, so standardize features first.
- **`gamma`** in the RBF kernel controls how far one example's influence reaches; too high overfits.

```python
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

svm = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), SVC(kernel='rbf', C=1.0, gamma='scale'))
svm.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

## Why it matters for AI
Before deep learning, kernel SVMs were state-of-the-art for text and image classification, and they remain excellent on **small-to-medium, high-dimensional datasets** (like gene expression or TF-IDF text) where they often beat neural nets. The margin-maximization principle underpins modern ideas about generalization, and hinge loss reappears across ML. Understanding the kernel trick also demystifies why *similarity functions* — the beating heart of embeddings and attention — are so central to AI.

examples.title: RBF-kernel SVM in a scaling pipeline
examples.code:
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)
X_tr, X_te, y_tr, y_te = train_test_split(X, y, random_state=0)

model = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), SVC(kernel='rbf', C=1.0))
model.fit(X_tr, y_tr)
print('test accuracy:', round(model.score(X_te, y_te), 4))
print('n support vectors:', model.named_steps['svc'].n_support_)

examples.title: Effect of C on the margin
examples.code:
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.datasets import load_wine
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

X, y = load_wine(return_X_y=True)
for c in [0.01, 0.1, 1, 10]:
    m = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), SVC(C=c))
    print(f'C={c:<5} cv acc={cross_val_score(m, X, y, cv=5).mean():.3f}')

quiz.text: What does an SVM maximize when choosing its decision boundary?
options: The margin, i.e., the distance from the boundary to the nearest points of each class
quiz.correct_answer: The margin, i.e., the distance from the boundary to the nearest points of each class
quiz.explanation: SVMs pick the maximum-margin hyperplane, which tends to generalize best because it stays as far as possible from both classes.

quiz.text: The technique that lets an SVM draw non-linear boundaries without explicitly computing high-dimensional coordinates is called the ______ trick.
quiz.explanation: Kernels replace dot products with a similarity function, implicitly operating in a higher-dimensional space efficiently.

quiz.text: What does a very large value of the hyperparameter C do?
options: Widens the margin and tolerates more misclassifications
Penalizes margin violations heavily, risking overfitting
Forces all points to be support vectors
quiz.correct_answer: Penalizes margin violations heavily, risking overfitting
quiz.explanation: Large C allows few violations, producing a tighter fit with higher variance; small C regularizes with a wider, more forgiving margin.

challenges.title: Average hinge loss
challenges.description: Compute the mean hinge loss for an SVM. Labels are +1 or -1, and predictions are raw scores. Hinge loss per sample is max(0, 1 - y*pred); return the average over all samples, rounded to 4 decimals.
hints: For each pair, compute margin = y * pred, then the loss is max(0, 1 - margin).
Sum the per-sample losses and divide by len(y_true) before rounding.

lessons.title: KNN
lessons.description: A lazy, instance-based method that classifies a point by the majority label of its k nearest neighbors.
lessons.content_markdown:
**K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** is the simplest intuition in ML made into an algorithm: to classify a new point, look at the `k` closest examples in the training data and let them vote. For regression, average their target values instead. There is essentially **no training** — the model just stores the data — which is why KNN is called a **lazy** or instance-based learner. All the work happens at prediction time, when it computes distances to every stored point.

## Key ideas
- **Distance metric**: usually Euclidean, sometimes Manhattan or cosine. Because it is distance-based, you **must standardize features** or a large-scale feature dominates.
- **Choosing k**: small k (e.g., 1) fits tightly and is noisy (high variance); large k smooths the boundary but can blur classes (high bias). Tune k with cross-validation; odd k avoids ties in binary problems.
- **Weighting**: `weights='distance'` lets closer neighbors count more than far ones.
- **The curse of dimensionality**: in high dimensions distances become nearly equal and 'nearest' loses meaning — KNN degrades, so reduce dimensions first.
- **Cost**: prediction is O(n) per query on n stored points; large datasets need KD-trees, ball-trees, or approximate nearest-neighbor indexes.

```python
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline

knn = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(),
                    KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=5, weights='distance'))
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

## Why it matters for AI
KNN is a strong, honest baseline and a superb teaching tool for the bias–variance trade-off. More importantly, its central operation — *find the nearest items in a feature space* — is exactly what modern **vector databases** and **retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)** do at scale: they embed text or images with a neural network, then run (approximate) nearest-neighbor search. Mastering KNN gives you the mental model for semantic search, recommendation, and similarity systems that power much of today's AI.

examples.title: KNN classifier with feature scaling
examples.code:
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
X_tr, X_te, y_tr, y_te = train_test_split(X, y, random_state=0)

knn = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=5))
knn.fit(X_tr, y_tr)
print('test accuracy:', round(knn.score(X_te, y_te), 4))

examples.title: Pick the best k with cross-validation
examples.code:
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_wine
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline

X, y = load_wine(return_X_y=True)
for k in [1, 3, 5, 7, 15]:
    model = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=k))
    print(f'k={k:>2}  cv acc={cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5).mean():.3f}')

quiz.text: Why is KNN called a 'lazy' learner?
options: It does no real training and defers all computation to prediction time
It only works on small k
quiz.correct_answer: It does no real training and defers all computation to prediction time
quiz.explanation: KNN simply stores the training data; distances and voting are computed only when a query arrives.

quiz.text: What happens as you increase k in KNN?
options: The boundary gets smoother, raising bias and lowering variance
quiz.correct_answer: The boundary gets smoother, raising bias and lowering variance
quiz.explanation: Averaging over more neighbors smooths predictions; too large a k oversmooths and blurs the classes.

quiz.text: The phenomenon where distances become nearly equal in high-dimensional space, degrading KNN, is called the curse of ______.
quiz.explanation: In many dimensions, nearest and farthest neighbors have similar distances, so 'nearest' carries little information.

challenges.title: K-nearest-neighbors classifier
challenges.description: Implement KNN classification from scratch. Given training points, their labels, a query point, and k, return the predicted label by majority vote of the k nearest training points (Euclidean distance). Break vote ties by smallest label.
challenges.starter_code:
import math
from collections import Counter

def knn_classify(train, labels, query, k):
    # Return the majority label among the k nearest training points to query.
    pass
hints: Pair each training point's distance to the query with its label, then sort by distance.
Take the first k labels, tally with Counter, and break ties by choosing the smallest label among those with the top count.

lessons.title: Cross Validation
lessons.description: A resampling technique that estimates true generalization by rotating which data is used for testing.
lessons.content_markdown:
A single train/test split gives you one noisy estimate of performance that depends heavily on *which* rows happened to land in the test set. **Cross-validation (CV)** fixes this by rotating the test set across the data so every point is evaluated exactly once, yielding a far more stable and trustworthy score.

The standard method is **k-fold cross-validation**: split the data into `k` equal folds; for each fold, train on the other `k−1` folds and test on the held-out one; then average the `k` scores. With `k=5`, every model trains on 80% and tests on a different 20% five times.

## Key ideas
- **Stratified k-fold** preserves the class proportions in each fold — essential for imbalanced classification, and scikit-learn's default for classifiers.
- **The golden rule — no leakage**: any preprocessing that learns from data (scaling, imputation, feature selection) must be fit *inside* each fold, never on the whole dataset first. Use a `Pipeline` so CV re-fits it correctly every fold.
- **Choosing k**: 5 or 10 are typical; larger k means less bias but more compute. **Leave-One-Out** (k = n) is the extreme.
- **Grouped / time-series CV**: when rows are correlated (same patient, or ordered in time), use `GroupKFold` or `TimeSeriesSplit` so the test set is genuinely unseen.
- **Hyperparameter tuning** with `GridSearchCV` wraps CV to pick the best settings honestly.

```python
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
scores = cross_val_score(pipeline, X, y, cv=5, scoring='accuracy')
print(scores.mean(), scores.std())   # report mean ± spread
```

## Why it matters for AI
Cross-validation is the discipline that keeps you honest. Nearly every reported result and every hyperparameter choice in serious ML rests on CV; skipping it — or leaking test information into training — is the single most common way engineers fool themselves with a model that collapses in production. The habit of fitting all preprocessing inside the fold scales directly to how you must validate deep-learning pipelines and LLM systems.

examples.title: Stratified cross-validation with a leak-proof pipeline
examples.code:
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer

X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)
pipe = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), LogisticRegression(max_iter=5000))
cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=0)
scores = cross_val_score(pipe, X, y, cv=cv)
print('accuracy:', round(scores.mean(), 4), '+/-', round(scores.std(), 4))

examples.title: Grid search chooses hyperparameters via CV
examples.code:
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.datasets import load_wine

X, y = load_wine(return_X_y=True)
grid = {'C': [0.1, 1, 10], 'gamma': ['scale', 0.01]}
search = GridSearchCV(SVC(), grid, cv=5).fit(X, y)
print('best params:', search.best_params_)
print('best cv score:', round(search.best_score_, 4))

quiz.text: In 5-fold cross-validation, how is the final score obtained?
options: Take the best of the 5 test scores
Average the test scores from all 5 held-out folds
quiz.correct_answer: Average the test scores from all 5 held-out folds
quiz.explanation: Each fold serves once as the test set; averaging the 5 scores gives a stable estimate of generalization.

quiz.text: Why must feature scaling be fit inside each CV fold rather than on the full dataset first?
options: To prevent data leakage from the test fold into training
To reduce the number of folds
quiz.correct_answer: To prevent data leakage from the test fold into training
quiz.explanation: Fitting the scaler on all data lets test-fold statistics influence training, inflating scores and breaking the honesty of the estimate.

quiz.text: The variant of k-fold that preserves each class's proportion within every fold is called ______ k-fold.
quiz.explanation: Stratified k-fold keeps class ratios consistent across folds, which is important for imbalanced classification.

challenges.title: Generate k-fold test indices
challenges.description: Split the indices 0..n-1 into k contiguous folds and return the list of test-index lists. Like scikit-learn, the first (n mod k) folds should be one element larger so all indices are used.
hints: Base fold size is n // k; the first (n % k) folds get one extra index.
Walk a running 'start' pointer and slice range(start, start+size) for each fold.
challenges.solution_code:
def kfold_indices(n, k):
    base, rem = divmod(n, k)
    folds = []
    start = 0
    for i in range(k):
        size = base + (1 if i < rem else 0)
        folds.append(list(range(start, start + size)))
        start += size
    return folds

lessons.title: Metrics
lessons.description: Choosing the right numeric measure of model quality for the problem and its costs.
lessons.content_markdown:
A model is only as good as the metric you judge it by — and the wrong metric hides serious failures. The right choice depends on the task and on the *cost of different mistakes*.

For **classification**, start from the **confusion matrix**: True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, False Negatives. From it come:
- **Accuracy** = correct / total. Fine when classes are balanced, dangerously misleading when they aren't.
- **Precision** = TP / (TP + FP): of everything flagged positive, how much was right. Care about this when false alarms are costly (e.g., blocking legitimate email).
- **Recall (sensitivity)** = TP / (TP + FN): of all real positives, how many you caught. Care about this when misses are costly (e.g., missing a fraud or a tumor).
- **F1 score** = harmonic mean of precision and recall, `2·P·R/(P+R)` — a single balanced number when both matter.
- **ROC-AUC** measures ranking quality across all thresholds; **PR-AUC** is better under heavy imbalance.

For **regression**: **MAE** (average absolute error, robust, same units), **RMSE** (penalizes big errors more, same units), and **R²** (fraction of variance explained).

## Key ideas
- **Precision and recall trade off**: lowering the decision threshold raises recall but lowers precision.
- **Match the metric to the business cost**, not to whatever looks highest.
- **Always compare to a baseline** (majority class, or predicting the mean) so a number has context.

```python
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix
print(confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred))
print(classification_report(y_true, y_pred))  # precision, recall, F1 per class
```

## Why it matters for AI
Every model decision, A/B test, and leaderboard rests on a metric; picking the wrong one is how teams ship models that score 99% and fail in the real world. In imbalanced, high-stakes AI — fraud, medical, safety, moderation — precision/recall and their trade-off drive threshold choices that have direct human and financial consequences. Fluency with these measures is what turns 'the model works' into a defensible, quantified claim.

examples.title: Full classification report
examples.code:
from sklearn.metrics import precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, confusion_matrix

y_true = [1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0]
y_pred = [1, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0]

print('confusion matrix:\n', confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred))
print('precision:', round(precision_score(y_true, y_pred), 4))
print('recall:   ', round(recall_score(y_true, y_pred), 4))
print('f1:       ', round(f1_score(y_true, y_pred), 4))

examples.title: Regression metrics
examples.code:
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error, r2_score
import numpy as np

y_true = np.array([3.0, -0.5, 2.0, 7.0])
y_pred = np.array([2.5, 0.0, 2.0, 8.0])

print('MAE :', round(mean_absolute_error(y_true, y_pred), 4))
print('RMSE:', round(np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_true, y_pred)), 4))
print('R^2 :', round(r2_score(y_true, y_pred), 4))

quiz.text: For a cancer-screening model where missing a real case is catastrophic, which metric should you prioritize?
quiz.explanation: Recall = TP/(TP+FN) measures how many true positives you catch, so maximizing it minimizes dangerous missed cases.

quiz.text: The metric that is the harmonic mean of precision and recall is the ______ score.
quiz.explanation: F1 = 2·P·R/(P+R) balances precision and recall into a single number, low if either is low.

quiz.text: What generally happens to precision when you lower the decision threshold to increase recall?
quiz.explanation: Flagging more items positive catches more true positives (higher recall) but also more false positives, lowering precision.

challenges.title: Binary F1 score
challenges.description: Compute the F1 score for binary predictions where 1 is the positive class and 0 is negative. Return 0.0 if precision or recall is undefined (zero denominator). Round to 4 decimals.
hints: Count TP (true=1,pred=1), FP (true=0,pred=1), FN (true=1,pred=0) in one pass.
precision=TP/(TP+FP), recall=TP/(TP+FN); if TP is 0 or a denominator is 0, return 0.0.

title: Deep Learning
description:
This phase takes you from the raw data structure of deep learning — the tensor — all the way to the two architectures that power modern vision and language systems: convolutional and recurrent networks. You will build a solid mental model of how a network turns numbers into predictions (forward pass), measures its mistakes (loss), and improves itself (backpropagation plus an optimizer), and you will express all of it in PyTorch, the framework used across research and industry. Understanding these mechanics — not just calling `model.fit()` — is what separates an AI engineer who can debug a failing training run from someone who is stuck. By the end you will be able to read a research diagram, translate it into working PyTorch, and reason about why a network learns or fails to learn. Projects to build: Handwritten Digit Recognition, Image Classifier, Sentiment Analysis.
Resources: PyTorch tutorials; Deep Learning (Goodfellow) for theory.

lessons.title: Tensors
lessons.description: A tensor is the fundamental multi-dimensional array that stores every number flowing through a neural network.
lessons.content_markdown:
A **tensor** is a generalization of scalars, vectors, and matrices to any number of dimensions. A scalar is a 0-D tensor, a vector is 1-D, a matrix is 2-D, and a batch of RGB images is a 4-D tensor with shape `(batch, channels, height, width)`. Everything a neural network touches — inputs, weights, activations, gradients — is a tensor. In PyTorch the `torch.Tensor` object adds two superpowers over a plain nested list: it runs vectorized math on a **GPU**, and it can track operations to compute gradients automatically.

## Key ideas
- **Shape and dtype** define a tensor: `x.shape` (e.g. `torch.Size([2, 3])`) and `x.dtype` (e.g. `torch.float32`). Shape mismatches are the #1 source of beginner bugs.
- **Broadcasting** lets tensors of different shapes combine: adding a `(3,)` bias to a `(4, 3)` matrix stretches the bias across all 4 rows without copying memory.
- **Vectorization**: one tensor operation replaces an explicit Python loop and runs orders of magnitude faster.
- **`requires_grad=True`** turns a tensor into a node in the autograd graph so PyTorch can differentiate through it.

```python
import torch

a = torch.tensor([[1., 2., 3.],
                  [4., 5., 6.]])   # shape (2, 3)
bias = torch.tensor([10., 20., 30.])  # shape (3,)
print((a + bias))            # broadcasting -> (2, 3)
print(a.matmul(a.T).shape)   # (2, 2) matrix multiply
```

```python
# Reshaping and reducing
x = torch.arange(12)          # tensor([0, 1, ..., 11])
grid = x.reshape(3, 4)        # 3 rows, 4 cols
print(grid.sum(dim=0))        # column sums -> shape (4,)
print(grid.mean(dtype=torch.float))
```

## Why it matters for AI
Deep learning frameworks are, at their core, tensor engines. Model weights are stored as tensors, a forward pass is a chain of tensor operations, and training data arrives in tensor **batches**. Fluency with shapes, broadcasting, and reshaping is the difference between debugging a cryptic `RuntimeError: mat1 and mat2 shapes cannot be multiplied` in seconds versus hours. Moving tensors to a GPU with `.to('cuda')` is what makes training large models feasible at all.

examples.title: Creating tensors and inspecting shape/dtype
examples.code:
import torch

scalar = torch.tensor(3.14)
vector = torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
matrix = torch.zeros(2, 3)
images = torch.randn(8, 3, 32, 32)  # batch of 8 RGB 32x32 images

for name, t in [('scalar', scalar), ('vector', vector),
                ('matrix', matrix), ('images', images)]:
    print(f'{name:7} shape={tuple(t.shape)} dtype={t.dtype} ndim={t.ndim}')

examples.title: GPU placement and broadcasting
examples.code:
import torch

device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
x = torch.ones(4, 3, device=device)
row = torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], device=device)
print((x * row))            # broadcast multiply across rows
print(x.sum().item())       # .item() pulls a Python float out

quiz.text: What is the shape of a standard batch of RGB images in PyTorch?
quiz.explanation: PyTorch convention for image batches is NCHW: batch, channels, height, width.

quiz.text: The mechanism that lets a (3,) bias be added to a (4, 3) matrix without copying is called ____.
quiz.explanation: Broadcasting virtually stretches a smaller tensor across a compatible dimension of a larger one.

quiz.text: Which attribute must be set for autograd to track operations on a tensor?
quiz.explanation: Setting requires_grad=True makes the tensor a node in the computation graph so gradients can be computed.

challenges.title: Matrix multiplication from scratch
challenges.description: Implement 2-D matrix multiplication using only nested lists, the operation at the heart of every neural network layer.
hints: The result has len(A) rows and len(B[0]) columns.
Each output cell [i][j] is the sum over k of A[i][k] * B[k][j].

lessons.title: Neural Networks
lessons.description: A neural network is a stack of linear layers and nonlinearities that maps inputs to predictions by composing simple weighted sums.
lessons.content_markdown:
A **neural network** is a function built by stacking layers. Each **linear (dense) layer** computes `y = Wx + b` — a weighted sum of its inputs plus a bias — and each layer is followed by a nonlinear **activation**. Stacking `linear -> activation -> linear -> activation ...` lets the network approximate extremely complex functions; the **universal approximation theorem** says even one hidden layer of sufficient width can approximate any continuous function. The values flowing between layers are called **activations**; the numbers being learned are the **weights** and **biases**, collectively the **parameters**.

## Key ideas
- A **layer** transforms a vector of size `in_features` into a vector of size `out_features` via a weight matrix of shape `(out_features, in_features)`.
- **Depth** (many layers) builds hierarchical features; **width** (units per layer) adds capacity.
- Without a nonlinear activation between them, stacked linear layers collapse into a single linear layer — the nonlinearity is what gives the network its power.
- In PyTorch you define a model by subclassing `nn.Module` and implementing `forward()`.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(784, 128)   # input -> hidden
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(128, 10)    # hidden -> 10 classes

    def forward(self, x):
        x = torch.relu(self.fc1(x))      # nonlinearity in between
        return self.fc2(x)               # raw logits

model = MLP()
out = model(torch.randn(4, 784))         # batch of 4
print(out.shape)                         # torch.Size([4, 10])
```

## Why it matters for AI
The multilayer perceptron (MLP) above is the skeleton behind your Handwritten Digit Recognition project: flatten a 28x28 image into 784 numbers, push it through two layers, and read off 10 class scores. Every advanced architecture — CNNs, transformers — is a specialized arrangement of these same linear-plus-nonlinear building blocks. Understanding that a network is just composed functions demystifies the field and lets you reason precisely about parameter counts, capacity, and where a model might overfit.

examples.title: A minimal trainable MLP in PyTorch
examples.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(4, 16),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(16, 3),
)

x = torch.randn(5, 4)          # 5 samples, 4 features each
logits = model(x)
print('output shape:', logits.shape)
n_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())
print('trainable parameters:', n_params)

examples.title: Counting the weights in one linear layer
examples.code:
import torch.nn as nn

layer = nn.Linear(in_features=784, out_features=128)
print('weight shape:', tuple(layer.weight.shape))  # (128, 784)
print('bias shape:', tuple(layer.bias.shape))      # (128,)
print('total params:', 784 * 128 + 128)            # weights + biases

quiz.text: Why do we place a nonlinear activation between two linear layers?
options: Because two stacked linear layers without it collapse into one linear layer
To reduce the number of parameters
quiz.correct_answer: Because two stacked linear layers without it collapse into one linear layer
quiz.explanation: Composing linear functions yields another linear function; the nonlinearity is what lets the network model complex relationships.

quiz.text: In PyTorch you define a custom model by subclassing nn.Module and implementing the ____ method.
quiz.explanation: The forward method specifies how input tensors flow through the layers to produce output.

quiz.text: An nn.Linear(784, 128) layer has how many weight parameters (excluding bias)?
quiz.explanation: The weight matrix is 128 x 784 = 100,352 values, plus 128 bias terms.

challenges.title: Dense layer forward pass
challenges.description: Compute the output of a fully-connected layer given inputs, per-neuron weights, and biases.
challenges.starter_code:
def dense_forward(inputs, weights, bias):
    # weights[j] holds the weight vector for neuron j; bias[j] its bias.
    # Return a list: output[j] = sum(inputs[i]*weights[j][i]) + bias[j]
    pass
hints: Loop over each neuron j, then over each input i.
Start each neuron's sum at bias[j] and accumulate the weighted inputs.

lessons.title: Activation Functions
lessons.description: Activation functions inject nonlinearity into a network, deciding how strongly each neuron fires.
lessons.content_markdown:
An **activation function** is applied element-wise to a layer's output to introduce nonlinearity. Without it, no matter how many layers you stack, the model can only represent linear relationships. Different activations shape *how* signals propagate and *how well* gradients flow backward during training.

## Key ideas
- **ReLU** `max(0, x)`: the default for hidden layers. Cheap, avoids saturating for positive inputs, but can 'die' (output a constant 0) if a neuron only ever sees negatives.
- **Sigmoid** `1 / (1 + e^-x)`: squashes to (0, 1), used for binary probabilities. Saturates at the extremes, causing **vanishing gradients** in deep nets.
- **Tanh** `(e^x - e^-x)/(e^x + e^-x)`: squashes to (-1, 1), zero-centered, common in RNNs.
- **Softmax**: converts a vector of logits into a probability distribution that sums to 1 — used at the output of multi-class classifiers.
- **Leaky ReLU** allows a small negative slope to prevent dead neurons.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

x = torch.tensor([-2.0, -0.5, 0.0, 1.0, 3.0])
print(F.relu(x))       # tensor([0., 0., 0., 1., 3.])
print(torch.sigmoid(x))
print(torch.tanh(x))

logits = torch.tensor([2.0, 1.0, 0.1])
print(F.softmax(logits, dim=0))  # sums to 1.0
```

## Why it matters for AI
Choosing the right activation is a real engineering decision. ReLU (and variants) dominate hidden layers of CNNs and MLPs because their gradient is a clean 0 or 1, keeping backpropagation stable in deep networks. Sigmoid and softmax live at the output because they produce interpretable probabilities — exactly what you need for the Sentiment Analysis (positive/negative) and Image Classifier (multi-class) projects. Recognizing that saturating activations cause vanishing gradients explains one of the historical reasons deep networks were hard to train before ReLU became popular.

examples.title: Comparing activations element-wise
examples.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

x = torch.linspace(-3, 3, steps=7)
print('input  :', x.tolist())
print('relu   :', F.relu(x).tolist())
print('sigmoid:', [round(v, 3) for v in torch.sigmoid(x).tolist()])
print('tanh   :', [round(v, 3) for v in torch.tanh(x).tolist()])

examples.title: Softmax turns logits into probabilities
examples.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

logits = torch.tensor([1.5, 2.5, 0.2, -1.0])
probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=0)
print('probabilities:', [round(p, 4) for p in probs.tolist()])
print('sum:', round(probs.sum().item(), 6))     # 1.0
print('predicted class:', torch.argmax(probs).item())

quiz.text: Which activation is the standard default for hidden layers of a deep network?
quiz.explanation: ReLU is cheap and its 0/1 gradient keeps backpropagation stable in deep networks, making it the common default.

quiz.text: The activation that converts a vector of logits into a probability distribution summing to 1 is ____.
quiz.explanation: Softmax exponentiates and normalizes logits so the outputs are positive and sum to 1.

quiz.text: Why can sigmoid cause problems in deep networks?
options: It is too expensive to compute
It saturates and causes vanishing gradients
quiz.correct_answer: It saturates and causes vanishing gradients
quiz.explanation: For large-magnitude inputs sigmoid's gradient approaches zero, so gradients shrink toward zero across many layers.

challenges.title: Implement ReLU over a list
challenges.description: Apply the ReLU activation element-wise to a list of numbers.
hints: Use a list comprehension over the input.
Keep x when it is positive, otherwise use 0.
challenges.solution_code:
def relu(xs):
    return [x if x > 0 else 0 for x in xs]

lessons.title: Loss Functions
lessons.description: A loss function measures how wrong a model's predictions are, producing the single number that training tries to minimize.
lessons.content_markdown:
A **loss function** (or cost function) maps a batch of predictions and their true targets to a single scalar measuring error. Training a network *is* the process of adjusting parameters to make this number as small as possible. The choice of loss encodes what 'good' means for your task.

## Key ideas
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** `mean((pred - target)^2)`: the standard loss for **regression** (predicting continuous values). Penalizes large errors quadratically.
- **Cross-Entropy Loss**: the standard loss for **classification**. It compares the predicted probability distribution against the true class and heavily punishes confident wrong answers. In PyTorch, `nn.CrossEntropyLoss` conveniently takes raw **logits** and applies softmax internally — do not softmax first.
- **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)**: cross-entropy's two-class special case, used for the Sentiment Analysis project.
- A loss must be **differentiable** so gradients can be computed with respect to every parameter.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Regression
mse = nn.MSELoss()
pred = torch.tensor([2.5, 0.0, 2.0])
target = torch.tensor([3.0, -0.5, 2.0])
print(mse(pred, target).item())    # 0.0833...

# Classification (logits, not probabilities)
ce = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
logits = torch.tensor([[2.0, 0.5, 0.1]])   # one sample, 3 classes
true = torch.tensor([0])                    # correct class index
print(ce(logits, true).item())
```

## Why it matters for AI
The loss is the objective the entire optimization procedure chases, so picking the wrong one silently sabotages a project. Using MSE for classification, or forgetting that `CrossEntropyLoss` wants raw logits and applying softmax twice, are classic bugs that make loss curves flatten or explode. Watching the loss value fall during training is your primary diagnostic: if it does not decrease, learning is not happening, and you check the loss, learning rate, and data before anything else.

examples.title: MSE for regression
examples.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()
predictions = torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0])
targets     = torch.tensor([1.5, 1.5, 3.5, 3.0])
loss = loss_fn(predictions, targets)
print('MSE:', round(loss.item(), 4))   # mean of squared diffs

examples.title: Cross-entropy for multi-class classification
examples.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
# batch of 2 samples, 3 possible classes, raw logits
logits = torch.tensor([[2.0, 0.1, 0.0],
                       [0.2, 0.3, 3.0]])
targets = torch.tensor([0, 2])   # correct class indices
print('loss:', round(loss_fn(logits, targets).item(), 4))

quiz.text: Which loss function is standard for a multi-class classification task?
quiz.explanation: Cross-entropy compares predicted class probabilities to the true class and is the default for classification.

quiz.text: PyTorch's nn.CrossEntropyLoss expects raw ____ as input, not softmaxed probabilities.
quiz.explanation: CrossEntropyLoss applies log-softmax internally, so you pass raw logits to avoid applying softmax twice.

quiz.text: A loss function must be differentiable primarily so that:
options: It runs faster on a GPU
Gradients can be computed to update the parameters
It always returns a positive number
quiz.correct_answer: Gradients can be computed to update the parameters
quiz.explanation: Gradient-based optimization needs derivatives of the loss with respect to every parameter.

challenges.title: Mean squared error
challenges.description: Compute the mean squared error between predictions and targets.
hints: Zip pred and target together to pair them up.
Square each difference, sum them, then divide by the count.
challenges.solution_code:
def mse(pred, target):
    return sum((p - t) ** 2 for p, t in zip(pred, target)) / len(pred)

lessons.title: Optimizers
lessons.description: An optimizer is the algorithm that updates a network's parameters using their gradients to reduce the loss.
lessons.content_markdown:
An **optimizer** decides *how* to change each parameter once backpropagation has computed its gradient. The simplest rule is **gradient descent**: nudge each parameter a small step in the direction that most decreases the loss, i.e. opposite its gradient. The step size is the **learning rate**, the single most important hyperparameter in deep learning.

## Key ideas
- **Update rule (SGD):** `param = param - learning_rate * gradient`.
- **Learning rate** too high -> loss diverges or oscillates; too low -> training crawls. Typical starting values are 0.1 to 0.001.
- **Stochastic** gradient descent uses one mini-batch at a time instead of the whole dataset, giving noisy but fast updates.
- **Momentum** accumulates a velocity of past gradients to power through flat regions and small local bumps.
- **Adam** combines momentum with per-parameter adaptive learning rates and is the go-to default for most projects.
- The canonical PyTorch loop is: `optimizer.zero_grad()` -> `loss.backward()` -> `optimizer.step()`. Forgetting `zero_grad()` makes gradients accumulate across steps, a classic bug.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Linear(4, 1)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

for epoch in range(100):
    pred = model(torch.randn(16, 4))
    loss = loss_fn(pred, torch.randn(16, 1))
    optimizer.zero_grad()   # clear old gradients
    loss.backward()         # compute new gradients
    optimizer.step()        # update parameters
```

## Why it matters for AI
The optimizer and its learning rate govern whether training converges, diverges, or stalls — they are what you tune first when a model will not learn. Knowing that Adam adapts per-parameter step sizes explains why it often 'just works' with little tuning, while plain SGD with momentum can generalize better once you invest in a learning-rate schedule. Mastering the `zero_grad -> backward -> step` ordering is essential; every training script you write in this phase follows it.

examples.title: The standard PyTorch training step
examples.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

model = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(2, 8), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(8, 1))
opt = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.05, momentum=0.9)
loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

X = torch.randn(32, 2)
y = (X[:, :1] * 2 + 1)          # a simple linear target
for step in range(5):
    loss = loss_fn(model(X), y)
    opt.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    opt.step()
    print(f'step {step} loss {loss.item():.4f}')

examples.title: One manual SGD update by hand
examples.code:
import torch

w = torch.tensor([1.0], requires_grad=True)
lr = 0.1
loss = (w - 5.0) ** 2          # minimized at w = 5
loss.backward()                # dloss/dw = 2*(w-5) = -8
with torch.no_grad():
    w -= lr * w.grad           # w = 1 - 0.1*(-8) = 1.8
print(w.item())                # moved toward 5

quiz.text: What does the basic SGD update rule do to each parameter?
options: Adds the learning rate times the gradient
Subtracts the learning rate times the gradient
Multiplies the parameter by the gradient
Sets the parameter equal to the gradient
quiz.correct_answer: Subtracts the learning rate times the gradient
quiz.explanation: Moving opposite the gradient decreases the loss: param = param - lr * gradient.

quiz.text: You must call optimizer.____ before loss.backward() to prevent gradients from accumulating across steps.
quiz.explanation: PyTorch accumulates gradients by default, so zero_grad() clears them before each new backward pass.

quiz.text: Which optimizer combines momentum with per-parameter adaptive learning rates and is a common default?
quiz.explanation: Adam maintains adaptive per-parameter step sizes plus momentum, making it robust with little tuning.

challenges.title: One SGD update step
challenges.description: Apply a single gradient descent update to a list of parameters.
hints: Zip params and grads together.
Subtract lr * grad from each parameter.
challenges.solution_code:
def sgd_step(params, grads, lr):
    return [p - lr * g for p, g in zip(params, grads)]

lessons.title: Backpropagation
lessons.description: Backpropagation is the algorithm that efficiently computes the gradient of the loss with respect to every parameter using the chain rule.
lessons.content_markdown:
**Backpropagation** is how a network learns. After the forward pass produces a loss, backprop computes how much each parameter contributed to that loss — its **gradient** — by applying the **chain rule** of calculus backward through the computation graph, layer by layer. It reuses intermediate results so that computing all gradients costs about the same as one forward pass, which is what makes training deep networks tractable.

## Key ideas
- Every operation records how to compute its **local gradient**; backprop multiplies these along each path from loss to parameter (the chain rule).
- For a chain `L(y)`, `y = f(x)`, `x = g(w)`: `dL/dw = dL/dy * dy/dx * dx/dw`.
- **Autograd** in PyTorch builds this graph automatically as you run the forward pass; calling `loss.backward()` populates every tensor's `.grad`.
- Gradients flow *backward*: output layer first, input layer last — hence 'back'-propagation.
- Concrete example: for `y = w*x + b` and loss `L = (y - t)^2`, the chain rule gives `dL/dw = 2*(y - t)*x` and `dL/db = 2*(y - t)`.

```python
import torch

x = torch.tensor(2.0)
w = torch.tensor(3.0, requires_grad=True)
b = torch.tensor(1.0, requires_grad=True)
t = torch.tensor(10.0)

y = w * x + b            # forward: 3*2 + 1 = 7
loss = (y - t) ** 2      # (7 - 10)^2 = 9
loss.backward()          # backprop
print(w.grad.item())     # 2*(7-10)*2 = -12
print(b.grad.item())     # 2*(7-10)   = -6
```

## Why it matters for AI
Backpropagation is *the* enabling idea of modern deep learning; without an efficient way to get gradients, none of it works. Even though PyTorch's autograd hides the mechanics, understanding it lets you diagnose real failures: **vanishing gradients** (signals shrink to zero across deep stacks, so early layers stop learning) and **exploding gradients** (they blow up, so loss becomes NaN). Knowing that gradients are products of local derivatives explains why activation choice, weight initialization, and architectures like residual connections matter so much.

examples.title: Autograd computes gradients automatically
examples.code:
import torch

w = torch.tensor([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], requires_grad=True)
x = torch.tensor([4.0, 5.0, 6.0])

y = (w * x).sum()        # 1*4 + 2*5 + 3*6 = 32
y.backward()             # dy/dw = x
print(w.grad)            # tensor([4., 5., 6.])

examples.title: Chain rule through two operations
examples.code:
import torch

a = torch.tensor(2.0, requires_grad=True)
b = a ** 3               # b = 8,  db/da = 3*a^2 = 12
c = 5 * b                # c = 40, dc/db = 5
c.backward()             # dc/da = dc/db * db/da = 5 * 12
print(a.grad.item())     # 60.0

quiz.text: Which calculus rule is the mathematical core of backpropagation?
quiz.explanation: Backprop multiplies local derivatives along each path from the loss to a parameter, which is the chain rule.

quiz.text: Calling loss.____() in PyTorch triggers backpropagation and fills each tensor's .grad attribute.
quiz.explanation: loss.backward() traverses the autograd graph in reverse to compute all gradients.

quiz.text: When gradient signals shrink toward zero as they propagate through many layers, this is called:
quiz.explanation: Repeated multiplication by small local gradients drives the signal toward zero, stalling early-layer learning.

challenges.description: Given inputs to a one-neuron model y = w*x + b with squared-error loss against target t, return the gradients (dL/dw, dL/db).
challenges.starter_code:
def grad(x, w, b, t):
    # L = (y - t)**2 where y = w*x + b
    # Return a tuple (dL/dw, dL/db)
    pass
hints: Compute y first, then the shared factor 2*(y - t).
dL/dw multiplies that factor by x; dL/db is just that factor.

lessons.title: CNN
lessons.description: Convolutional Neural Networks use sliding filters to detect spatial patterns, making them the backbone of computer vision.
lessons.content_markdown:
A **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** is a network specialized for grid-like data such as images. Instead of connecting every pixel to every neuron (which would need millions of weights), a CNN slides small learnable **filters (kernels)** across the image, computing dot products to produce **feature maps**. This gives two crucial properties: **parameter sharing** (the same filter is reused everywhere, drastically cutting weights) and **translation invariance** (a pattern is detected wherever it appears).

## Key ideas
- A **convolution** slides a kernel (e.g. 3x3) over the input; each position outputs one weighted sum. Early filters learn edges; deeper ones learn textures, then object parts.
- **Stride** controls how far the kernel jumps; **padding** adds a border so output size can match input size.
- **Pooling** (e.g. max-pool 2x2) downsamples feature maps, shrinking spatial size and adding robustness.
- A typical block is `Conv -> ReLU -> Pool`, stacked several times, then flattened into linear layers for classification.
- Output size of a valid convolution: `(W - K)/stride + 1`.

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class SmallCNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(1, 16, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
        self.pool  = nn.MaxPool2d(2)
        self.fc    = nn.Linear(16 * 14 * 14, 10)

    def forward(self, x):            # x: (batch, 1, 28, 28)
        x = self.pool(torch.relu(self.conv1(x)))  # -> (batch,16,14,14)
        x = x.flatten(1)
        return self.fc(x)
```

## Why it matters for AI
CNNs are exactly what you will use for the Handwritten Digit Recognition and Image Classifier projects, and they revolutionized computer vision — from ImageNet breakthroughs to medical imaging and self-driving perception. Understanding why convolution beats a plain MLP on images (far fewer parameters, built-in spatial priors) teaches a deep lesson: encoding the right **inductive bias** for your data type is often more powerful than adding raw capacity. The same idea — local, shared, structured connections — echoes throughout modern architecture design.

examples.title: A convolutional layer changes channels and spatial size
examples.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

conv = nn.Conv2d(in_channels=3, out_channels=8,
                 kernel_size=3, stride=1, padding=1)
x = torch.randn(4, 3, 32, 32)      # 4 RGB images
out = conv(x)
print('output shape:', tuple(out.shape))   # (4, 8, 32, 32)
print('params:', sum(p.numel() for p in conv.parameters()))

examples.title: Max pooling downsamples a feature map
examples.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

pool = nn.MaxPool2d(kernel_size=2)
feat = torch.randn(1, 8, 28, 28)
print('before:', tuple(feat.shape))   # (1, 8, 28, 28)
print('after :', tuple(pool(feat).shape))  # (1, 8, 14, 14)

quiz.text: What key property lets a CNN detect the same pattern anywhere in an image with far fewer weights?
options: Parameter sharing across the sliding kernel
quiz.correct_answer: Parameter sharing across the sliding kernel
quiz.explanation: One kernel is reused at every position, so the same feature detector applies everywhere while cutting parameters.

quiz.text: The operation that downsamples a feature map by keeping the maximum in each window is called max ____.
quiz.explanation: Max pooling reduces spatial dimensions and adds translation robustness by keeping the strongest activation per window.

quiz.text: A 6x6 input convolved with a 3x3 kernel, stride 1, no padding, produces what spatial output size?
quiz.explanation: (W - K)/stride + 1 = (6 - 3)/1 + 1 = 4, so the output is 4x4.
