    # If all weights are zero, return a list of 0.0 of the same length.
    pass
hints: The largest magnitude weight maps to +/- qmax, which sets the scale.
Reconstruct each weight as round(w/scale) * scale; the result differs slightly from the input (that is the quantization error).

title: AI Engineering
description: This phase teaches the backend engineering skills you need to turn a model or prompt into a real, shippable product that thousands of users can hit reliably. You will build HTTP APIs with FastAPI, package and deploy them with Docker, cache and rate-limit with Redis, store users and embeddings in PostgreSQL, secure endpoints with authentication, design clean REST contracts, run slow model calls as background jobs, and stream tokens live over WebSockets. These are exactly the pieces that sit between your Python inference code and a paying customer, and they are what separate a notebook demo from a production AI service. Master them and you can independently ship, scale, and operate AI features end to end. Projects to build: AI SaaS, AI CRM, AI Support Bot, AI Email Writer. Resources: FastAPI, Docker, Redis, PostgreSQL official docs.

lessons.title: FastAPI
lessons.description: Build fast, typed, self-documenting HTTP APIs in Python that serve your AI models.
lessons.content_markdown:
FastAPI is a modern Python web framework for building HTTP APIs. It uses standard type hints to validate incoming requests, serialize responses, and auto-generate interactive OpenAPI docs. It runs on ASGI (via Uvicorn), so a single worker can handle many concurrent requests using `async`/`await` — critical when each request spends most of its time waiting on a slow model or an external LLM API.

The core loop: you declare a path operation with a decorator, type its parameters, and FastAPI does the parsing, validation, and documentation for you. Request bodies are described with Pydantic models, which reject malformed input with a clear 422 error before your code ever runs.

## Key ideas
- **Path operations**: `@app.get("/items/{id}")` maps HTTP method + URL to a function.
- **Pydantic models** validate and coerce JSON bodies into typed Python objects.
- **`async def`** lets one process serve many slow requests concurrently — ideal for `await openai_call()`.
- **Dependency injection** (`Depends`) shares logic like auth, DB sessions, or a loaded model across routes.
- **Automatic docs** at `/docs` (Swagger UI) and `/redoc`, generated from your types.

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class Prompt(BaseModel):
    text: str
    max_tokens: int = 256

@app.post("/generate")
async def generate(p: Prompt):
    # p is already validated; p.text is guaranteed to be a str
    reply = await call_model(p.text, p.max_tokens)
    return {"completion": reply}
```

Because the response is a dict/Pydantic object, FastAPI serializes it to JSON and sets the content type automatically. Errors raised as `HTTPException(status_code=404)` become proper HTTP responses.

## Why it matters for AI
Almost every AI product is delivered as an API: a client sends a prompt, your server runs inference, and returns a result. FastAPI's async model is a natural fit because inference and third-party LLM calls are I/O-bound waits — you can serve hundreds of concurrent users without threads. Pydantic validation stops garbage input (wrong types, missing fields, oversized token counts) from reaching an expensive GPU call, and the auto-generated `/docs` page becomes a live playground your teammates and frontend devs use to test the model. It is the default backbone for serving models in 2026.

examples.title: Minimal AI inference endpoint
Run the server with Uvicorn
examples.code:
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate    # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
pip install "fastapi[standard]" uvicorn

# Run with hot reload for development
uvicorn main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

# Then open http://localhost:8000/docs for the interactive Swagger UI

quiz.text: Why is `async def` particularly valuable when serving LLM calls in FastAPI?
options: It makes the model run on multiple GPUs automatically
It lets one worker handle many concurrent requests while each awaits a slow I/O-bound call
It compiles the Python code to C for speed
It caches responses so identical prompts return instantly
quiz.correct_answer: It lets one worker handle many concurrent requests while each awaits a slow I/O-bound call
quiz.explanation: LLM/inference calls are I/O-bound waits, so async concurrency lets a single process serve many users without blocking.

quiz.text: What does FastAPI do when a request body fails Pydantic validation?
options: Silently drops the invalid fields and continues
Runs the endpoint anyway with default values
Returns a 422 Unprocessable Entity response before your function runs
quiz.correct_answer: Returns a 422 Unprocessable Entity response before your function runs
quiz.explanation: Validation happens up front; invalid input produces a structured 422 error and your endpoint code never executes.

quiz.text: FastAPI automatically generates interactive API documentation (Swagger UI) at the ______ URL path.
quiz.explanation: FastAPI serves Swagger UI at /docs and ReDoc at /redoc, both built from your type hints and Pydantic models.

lessons.title: Docker
lessons.description: Package your AI service and all its dependencies into a portable, reproducible container image.
lessons.content_markdown:
Docker packages your application together with its exact runtime, system libraries, and Python dependencies into a single **image** — a read-only, layered filesystem snapshot. A running instance of that image is a **container**: an isolated process that behaves identically on your laptop, a teammate's machine, and a production server. This kills the "works on my machine" problem, which is especially painful for AI code with heavy, version-sensitive dependencies like CUDA, PyTorch, and native BLAS libraries.

You describe how to build the image in a `Dockerfile`. Each instruction creates a cached layer, so ordering matters: copy and install dependencies before copying your source, so code changes don't invalidate the (slow) dependency layer.

## Key ideas
- **Image vs container**: the image is the template; the container is a live instance of it.
- **Layers & caching**: install deps in an early layer so rebuilds after a code edit are fast.
- **`.dockerignore`**: exclude `.venv`, `__pycache__`, model weights, and secrets from the build context.
- **Slim base images** (`python:3.12-slim`) keep images small; multi-stage builds drop build-only tools.
- **`EXPOSE` + `CMD`** declare the port and the process that runs when the container starts.

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
# Copy deps first so this layer caches across code changes
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
```

You build and run with `docker build -t my-ai .` and `docker run -p 8000:8000 my-ai`. Environment variables (`-e OPENAI_API_KEY=...`) inject secrets at runtime rather than baking them into the image. `docker compose` orchestrates multiple containers — your API plus Redis plus PostgreSQL — with one file.

## Why it matters for AI
AI deployments are dependency nightmares: a specific CUDA version, a pinned Torch build, native audio/vision libraries. A container freezes all of that into one artifact you can ship to any cloud, GPU box, or Kubernetes cluster and get identical behavior. It also makes horizontal scaling trivial — the orchestrator just launches more identical containers behind a load balancer. Every serious model-serving stack (Docker Compose, Kubernetes, SageMaker, Cloud Run) is built on container images.

examples.title: Multi-service stack with Docker Compose
examples.code:
services:
  api:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://app:secret@db:5432/app
      - REDIS_URL=redis://cache:6379/0
    depends_on:
      - db
      - cache
  cache:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_USER=app
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=secret
      - POSTGRES_DB=app
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  pgdata:

examples.title: Build, run, and inspect an image
examples.code:
# Build an image tagged 'my-ai' from the Dockerfile in the current dir
docker build -t my-ai .

# Run it, mapping host port 8000 to container port 8000, injecting a secret
docker run -p 8000:8000 -e OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-... my-ai

# List running containers and view logs
docker ps
docker logs -f <container_id>

# Bring up the whole stack (api + redis + postgres)
docker compose up --build

quiz.text: Why should you COPY requirements.txt and run pip install BEFORE copying the rest of your source code?
options: Because pip cannot find the source files otherwise
So the dependency layer stays cached and isn't rebuilt every time you edit application code
Because Docker requires requirements.txt to be the first file
To make the final image larger and more complete
quiz.correct_answer: So the dependency layer stays cached and isn't rebuilt every time you edit application code
quiz.explanation: Layer caching means an unchanged dependency step is reused; putting it before code copy avoids slow reinstalls on every edit.

quiz.text: A read-only template built from a Dockerfile is called an image, while a running instance of it is called a ______.
quiz.explanation: The image is the static template; a container is a live, isolated process launched from that image.

quiz.text: What is the recommended way to provide a secret like an API key to a container?
options: Hard-code it in the Dockerfile with ENV
Bake it into the image during docker build
Inject it at runtime via an environment variable (e.g. docker run -e)
Commit it to the source code that gets COPYied in
quiz.correct_answer: Inject it at runtime via an environment variable (e.g. docker run -e)
quiz.explanation: Secrets should be passed at runtime, not baked into the image where anyone with the image could read them.

lessons.title: Redis
lessons.description: Use an in-memory data store for caching, rate limiting, and fast ephemeral state in AI apps.
lessons.content_markdown:
Redis is an in-memory key-value data store. Because it keeps data in RAM, reads and writes take microseconds — orders of magnitude faster than a disk-backed database. It supports rich data types (strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets), atomic operations, per-key expiration (TTL), and pub/sub messaging. In AI backends it plays three starring roles: caching expensive results, rate limiting users, and holding short-lived session/conversation state.

The fundamental pattern is **cache-aside**: check Redis first; on a miss, compute the expensive result, store it with a TTL, and return it. For a deterministic LLM prompt or an embedding lookup, this can cut latency from seconds to milliseconds and slash your API bill.

## Key ideas
- **In-memory** = extremely fast, but data is ephemeral (configure persistence if you need durability).
- **TTL / expiry**: `SET key value EX 3600` auto-deletes after an hour — perfect for caches.
- **Atomic counters**: `INCR` is used to build simple rate limiters safely under concurrency.
- **Data types** beyond strings: hashes for objects, sorted sets for leaderboards/queues, lists for job queues.
- **Pub/Sub**: lightweight message broadcasting between processes.

```python
import redis, hashlib, json

r = redis.Redis(host="localhost", port=6379, decode_responses=True)

def cached_completion(prompt: str) -> str:
    key = "llm:" + hashlib.sha256(prompt.encode()).hexdigest()
    hit = r.get(key)
    if hit:
        return hit                 # cache hit: microseconds
    result = call_expensive_model(prompt)
    r.set(key, result, ex=3600)    # cache for 1 hour
    return result
```

A rate limiter is just as simple: `INCR` a per-user key, set its TTL on first hit, and reject when the count exceeds the limit within the window.

## Why it matters for AI
LLM calls are slow and expensive, so caching identical or near-identical requests directly reduces cost and latency — the single highest-leverage optimization in most AI apps. Redis also enforces fair usage: rate limiting stops one user from draining your token budget. Beyond that, it stores conversation history and session context between requests without a full database round-trip, and its list type is the backbone of task queues like Celery and RQ, which you'll use to run background inference jobs.

examples.title: Fixed-window rate limiter
Common Redis CLI commands
examples.code:
redis-cli

> SET greeting "hello" EX 60      # store with 60s expiry
OK
> GET greeting
"hello"
> TTL greeting                    # seconds left before expiry
(integer) 58
> INCR page_views                 # atomic counter
(integer) 1
> HSET user:1 name Ada plan pro   # store a hash (object)
(integer) 2
> HGETALL user:1

quiz.text: In the cache-aside pattern, what happens on a cache miss?
options: The request fails and returns an error to the user
You compute the expensive result, store it in Redis with a TTL, then return it
Redis automatically computes the value for you
The value is read from disk instead of RAM
quiz.correct_answer: You compute the expensive result, store it in Redis with a TTL, then return it
quiz.explanation: On a miss the application computes the value, writes it back to the cache with an expiry, and serves it.

quiz.text: Setting an expiration time on a key so Redis deletes it automatically is known as setting its ______.
quiz.explanation: TTL (time to live) tells Redis to auto-expire and remove the key after the given duration.

quiz.text: Which Redis command is the natural building block for a simple rate limiter under concurrency?
quiz.explanation: INCR atomically increments a counter, so concurrent requests can't corrupt the count while tracking usage per window.

lessons.title: PostgreSQL
lessons.description: Store users, application data, and even vector embeddings in a durable relational database.
lessons.content_markdown:
PostgreSQL is a powerful open-source relational database. Data lives in **tables** of typed columns; rows are queried and combined with SQL. Unlike Redis, Postgres is durable (data survives restarts) and transactional (ACID) — it guarantees that a group of writes either all succeed or all roll back, which you need for anything involving money, accounts, or billing. It also has a rich type system (JSONB, arrays, full-text search) and, crucially for AI, the **pgvector** extension for storing and searching embeddings.

You model your domain as tables with primary keys and foreign keys that enforce relationships. Indexes make lookups fast. You typically talk to Postgres from Python through a driver like `psycopg` or an ORM like SQLAlchemy, always using **parameterized queries** to prevent SQL injection.

## Key ideas
- **Relational schema**: tables, columns, primary keys, and foreign keys enforce structure and integrity.
- **ACID transactions**: `BEGIN … COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK` keep multi-step writes consistent.
- **Indexes** turn slow full-table scans into fast lookups on frequently queried columns.
- **JSONB** stores semi-structured data with indexing and querying support.
- **pgvector** adds a `vector` column type plus similarity operators (`<->`, `<=>`) for semantic search / RAG.

```sql
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;

CREATE TABLE documents (
    id        BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    content   TEXT NOT NULL,
    embedding VECTOR(1536)            -- OpenAI embedding size
);

-- Find the 5 most semantically similar documents to a query vector
SELECT id, content
FROM documents
ORDER BY embedding <-> '[0.01, -0.2, ...]'
LIMIT 5;
```

The `<->` operator computes L2 distance between vectors; `<=>` gives cosine distance. Adding an `ivfflat` or `hnsw` index makes this scale to millions of rows.

## Why it matters for AI
Every AI SaaS needs a durable system of record: users, subscriptions, chat history, usage logs. Postgres is the reliable default. But its superpower for AI is pgvector — you can store document embeddings right next to your relational data and run retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with a single `ORDER BY embedding <-> query` clause, joining semantic search to normal filters (by user, tenant, date) in one query. That means you often don't need a separate vector database at all: Postgres handles both your app data and your retrieval index.

examples.title: Parameterized query from Python (psycopg)
examples.code:
import psycopg

with psycopg.connect("postgresql://app:secret@localhost:5432/app") as conn:
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        # NEVER use f-strings for values -- use placeholders to stop SQL injection
        cur.execute(
            "INSERT INTO users (email, plan) VALUES (%s, %s) RETURNING id",
            ("ada@example.com", "pro"),
        )
        new_id = cur.fetchone()[0]
        conn.commit()

        cur.execute("SELECT email FROM users WHERE id = %s", (new_id,))
        print(cur.fetchone())

examples.title: Schema with a relationship and an index

quiz.text: What is the main reason to choose PostgreSQL over Redis for storing user accounts and billing data?
options: Postgres is faster for every possible operation
Postgres is durable and ACID-transactional, so critical data survives restarts and stays consistent
Postgres keeps all data only in RAM
quiz.correct_answer: Postgres is durable and ACID-transactional, so critical data survives restarts and stays consistent
quiz.explanation: Accounts and billing require durability and transactional guarantees, which a disk-backed ACID database provides.

quiz.text: The PostgreSQL extension that adds a vector column type for storing and searching embeddings is called ______.
quiz.explanation: pgvector adds the vector type and similarity operators, enabling semantic search and RAG inside Postgres.

quiz.text: Why should you use parameterized queries (placeholders) instead of building SQL with f-strings?
options: It prevents SQL injection by separating code from user-supplied values
It is required to open a database connection
quiz.correct_answer: It prevents SQL injection by separating code from user-supplied values
quiz.explanation: Placeholders send values separately from the SQL text, so malicious input can't alter the query structure.

lessons.title: Authentication
lessons.description: Verify who is calling your API and protect endpoints with passwords, tokens, and JWTs.
lessons.content_markdown:
Authentication answers "who are you?"; authorization answers "what are you allowed to do?". For an AI API you need both: to bill the right customer, enforce per-user rate limits, and keep tenants' data separate. The two dominant schemes are **API keys** (a long random secret a client sends on every request, great for machine-to-machine access) and **JWTs** (JSON Web Tokens, great for user login sessions).

Never store raw passwords. Hash them with a slow, salted algorithm like **bcrypt** or **argon2**, so a database leak doesn't expose credentials. On login, you verify the password against the stored hash and issue a **signed JWT** — a token containing claims (user id, expiry) plus a cryptographic signature. The client sends it in the `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header; your server verifies the signature (no database lookup needed) and trusts the claims.

## Key ideas
- **Hash, never store, passwords**: bcrypt/argon2 with a per-user salt; comparison is constant-time.
- **JWT structure**: `header.payload.signature`, base64url-encoded; the signature proves it wasn't tampered with.
- **Bearer tokens** travel in the `Authorization` header over HTTPS only.
- **Expiry (`exp`)**: short-lived access tokens plus longer refresh tokens limit damage from a leaked token.
- **The secret signing key** must stay server-side; anyone with it can forge tokens.

```python
import jwt, datetime  # PyJWT

SECRET = "keep-this-in-an-env-var"

def issue_token(user_id: int) -> str:
    payload = {
        "sub": str(user_id),
        "exp": datetime.datetime.utcnow() + datetime.timedelta(hours=1),
    }
    return jwt.encode(payload, SECRET, algorithm="HS256")

def verify_token(token: str) -> int:
    data = jwt.decode(token, SECRET, algorithms=["HS256"])  # raises if invalid/expired
    return int(data["sub"])
```

## Why it matters for AI
AI calls cost real money per token, so an unauthenticated endpoint is a direct financial liability — bots will find it and burn your budget. Authentication ties every inference request to a known user, which powers usage metering, billing, quotas, and per-tenant data isolation (a user must only see their own documents and chat history). It's also the foundation of multi-tenant AI SaaS: the same model server safely serves many customers because each request is authenticated and scoped to its owner.

examples.title: Hash and verify a password with bcrypt
Protecting a FastAPI route with a Bearer token

quiz.text: How should user passwords be stored in your database?
options: Hashed with a slow salted algorithm like bcrypt or argon2
quiz.correct_answer: Hashed with a slow salted algorithm like bcrypt or argon2
quiz.explanation: Salted one-way hashing means a database breach doesn't reveal the original passwords, and slowness resists brute force.

quiz.text: What does a JWT's cryptographic signature guarantee?
options: That the token's claims were not tampered with after signing
That the payload is encrypted and unreadable
That the user has admin privileges
quiz.correct_answer: That the token's claims were not tampered with after signing
quiz.explanation: The signature lets the server verify integrity and authenticity; note the payload is signed, not encrypted, so don't put secrets in it.

quiz.text: A JWT is typically sent to the server in the HTTP header named ______ (with the value 'Bearer <token>').
quiz.explanation: Clients place the token in the Authorization header using the Bearer scheme.

lessons.title: REST APIs
lessons.description: Design clean, predictable HTTP interfaces using resources, methods, and status codes.
lessons.content_markdown:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is a convention for designing HTTP APIs around **resources** — nouns like `users`, `conversations`, `documents` — that you manipulate with standard HTTP **methods**. Instead of inventing endpoint names like `/getUserData` and `/deleteThatUser`, you expose a resource URL and let the method express the action. This predictability is why REST is the default contract between frontends, mobile apps, and AI backends.

The method-to-action mapping is the heart of it: **GET** reads, **POST** creates, **PUT/PATCH** updates, **DELETE** removes. GET is *safe* (never changes state) and, along with PUT and DELETE, *idempotent* (repeating it has the same effect) — properties clients and proxies rely on for safe retries.

## Key ideas
- **Resources as URLs**: `/conversations`, `/conversations/42`, `/conversations/42/messages`.
- **Methods = verbs**: GET (read), POST (create), PUT/PATCH (update), DELETE (remove).
- **Status codes** convey outcome: 200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found, 429 Too Many Requests, 500 Server Error.
- **Statelessness**: each request carries everything needed (e.g. its auth token); the server keeps no per-client session in memory.
- **JSON bodies** for input/output; query params (`?limit=20&cursor=...`) for filtering and pagination.

```text
GET    /conversations            -> list conversations        (200)
POST   /conversations            -> create one                (201)
GET    /conversations/42         -> fetch conversation 42     (200/404)
POST   /conversations/42/messages-> add a message, run model  (201)
DELETE /conversations/42         -> delete it                 (204)
```

Good REST design also means consistent error shapes (`{"detail": "..."}`), versioning (`/v1/...`), and pagination for large lists rather than returning everything at once.

## Why it matters for AI
Your model is only useful once something can call it, and REST is the lingua franca every client understands. A well-designed REST surface — resources for conversations and messages, correct status codes, cursor pagination over long chat histories — makes your AI product easy to integrate, document (via OpenAPI), and evolve without breaking clients. Statelessness is what lets you scale: because no request depends on server memory, you can run many identical API containers behind a load balancer, and any one can handle any request. That is the exact model FastAPI encourages and the projects in this phase are built on.

examples.title: A resource-oriented FastAPI router
Calling the API with curl
examples.code:
# Create (POST) -> 201 Created
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/v1/conversations \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"title": "Trip planning"}'

# Read one (GET) -> 200 or 404
curl http://localhost:8000/v1/conversations/1

# List with a query param
curl "http://localhost:8000/v1/conversations?limit=10"

# Delete -> 204 No Content
curl -X DELETE http://localhost:8000/v1/conversations/1 -i

quiz.text: Which HTTP status code correctly indicates that a new resource was successfully created?
quiz.explanation: 201 signals that a POST successfully created a new resource; 200 is a generic success and 204 means success with no body.

quiz.text: What does it mean that REST APIs are 'stateless'?
options: The server never stores any data anywhere
Each request carries all the information needed to process it; the server keeps no per-client session in memory
The API cannot use a database
Requests must be sent in a fixed order
quiz.correct_answer: Each request carries all the information needed to process it; the server keeps no per-client session in memory
quiz.explanation: Statelessness lets any server instance handle any request, which is what makes horizontal scaling behind a load balancer possible.

quiz.text: To retrieve (read) a resource without changing any server state, you use the HTTP ______ method.
quiz.explanation: GET is the safe, read-only method; it must not modify state, which lets it be cached and safely retried.

lessons.title: Background Jobs
lessons.description: Run slow AI tasks asynchronously with a queue and workers so requests return instantly.
lessons.content_markdown:
Some AI work is too slow to finish inside a single HTTP request: generating a long document, embedding thousands of files, transcribing audio, or calling a chain of models. If you block the request until it finishes, the client times out and your web workers get tied up. The fix is **background jobs**: the API quickly enqueues a task and returns a job id (HTTP 202 Accepted); a separate pool of **worker** processes pulls tasks off a **queue** and executes them; the client later polls a status endpoint or receives a webhook/WebSocket update when it's done.

This decouples request latency from work duration. Your web tier stays fast and responsive while heavy lifting happens elsewhere, and you can scale workers independently of the API.

## Key ideas
- **Producer / queue / consumer**: the API produces tasks, a broker (often Redis) holds them, workers consume them.
- **Return 202 + job id**: respond immediately; expose `GET /jobs/{id}` for status/result.
- **Idempotency & retries**: workers may re-run a failed task, so make tasks safe to repeat.
- **Independent scaling**: add more workers for throughput without touching the API.
- **Tools**: Celery or RQ (Redis Queue) in Python; FastAPI's `BackgroundTasks` for lightweight fire-and-forget work.

```python
# tasks.py -- a Celery worker task
from celery import Celery

app = Celery("jobs", broker="redis://localhost:6379/0",
             backend="redis://localhost:6379/1")

@app.task
def summarize_document(doc_id: int) -> str:
    text = load_document(doc_id)      # slow
    return call_llm(f"Summarize:\n{text}")  # slow
```

The API enqueues with `summarize_document.delay(doc_id)`, which returns immediately with an `AsyncResult` whose id the client can poll. Run the worker with `celery -A tasks worker`.

## Why it matters for AI
Inference is the classic slow, bursty, occasionally-failing workload — exactly what queues were built for. Background jobs let you accept a request in milliseconds, smooth out traffic spikes (the queue buffers load instead of overwhelming your GPUs), retry transient model/API failures automatically, and scale the expensive inference tier separately from the cheap web tier. Batch pipelines — embedding a whole knowledge base for RAG, processing uploaded files, running nightly evaluations — are all background jobs. Nearly every production AI feature beyond a simple chat call relies on this pattern.

examples.title: FastAPI enqueues a job and exposes status
Start the broker and a worker
examples.code:
# 1) Redis acts as the message broker
docker run -d -p 6379:6379 redis:7-alpine

# 2) Install and launch a Celery worker that consumes the queue
pip install "celery[redis]"
celery -A tasks worker --loglevel=info --concurrency=4

# Scale throughput by launching more workers (or raising --concurrency)

quiz.text: Why should a long-running AI task (like transcribing audio) be handled as a background job instead of inside the HTTP request?
options: Because HTTP cannot transfer audio files
So the request returns immediately and web workers aren't tied up waiting for slow work
Because background jobs are always cheaper to run
quiz.correct_answer: So the request returns immediately and web workers aren't tied up waiting for slow work
quiz.explanation: Enqueuing the task decouples request latency from work duration, keeping the API responsive and workers free.

quiz.text: The HTTP status code conventionally returned when a request has been accepted for asynchronous processing but is not yet complete is ______.
quiz.explanation: 202 Accepted signals the work was queued; the client polls a status endpoint or awaits a callback for the result.

quiz.text: Because a worker may retry a failed task, background tasks should ideally be written to be:
options: Idempotent, so repeating them is safe
Limited to a single worker forever
quiz.correct_answer: Idempotent, so repeating them is safe
quiz.explanation: Retries mean a task can run more than once, so it must produce the same correct result without harmful side effects.

lessons.title: WebSockets
lessons.description: Stream tokens and live updates to clients over a persistent two-way connection.
lessons.content_markdown:
HTTP is request/response: the client asks, the server answers, the connection is done. That's a poor fit for a chatbot that should stream its answer word by word, or a dashboard that needs live job progress. **WebSockets** solve this with a single, long-lived, full-duplex TCP connection: after an initial HTTP `Upgrade` handshake, both client and server can push messages to each other at any time until either side closes it.

This is the mechanism behind the "typing" effect in modern AI chat UIs. Instead of waiting for the whole completion, the server forwards each token from the model the instant it's generated, so the user sees output appear in real time and perceives the app as fast even when total generation takes seconds.

## Key ideas
- **Persistent & bidirectional**: one connection, both sides send messages freely (unlike stateless HTTP).
- **Handshake**: starts as HTTP then upgrades to the `ws://` / `wss://` (TLS) protocol.
- **Token streaming**: push each generated chunk immediately for a live typing effect.
- **Stateful connections**: the server tracks who is connected — manage lifecycle (connect/disconnect) and clean up.
- **Alternative**: Server-Sent Events (SSE) is a simpler one-way (server->client) option when you don't need client->server streaming.

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect

app = FastAPI()

@app.websocket("/ws/chat")
async def chat(ws: WebSocket):
    await ws.accept()
    try:
        while True:
            prompt = await ws.receive_text()
            async for token in stream_model(prompt):  # async generator
                await ws.send_text(token)              # push each chunk
            await ws.send_text("[DONE]")
    except WebSocketDisconnect:
        pass  # client closed the tab; clean up here
```

The loop keeps the socket open so the user can send follow-up messages on the same connection, preserving conversational context on the server side.

## Why it matters for AI
Streaming is the defining UX of LLM products — nobody wants to stare at a spinner for ten seconds when tokens could be arriving continuously. WebSockets (or SSE) make that possible and also power live agent traces, tool-call progress, collaborative multi-user sessions, and real-time notifications when a background job finishes. Because the connection is bidirectional, the user can also interrupt or steer generation mid-stream. For any interactive AI feature, mastering realtime transport is what makes the product feel responsive and alive rather than clunky and batch-like.

examples.title: FastAPI server streaming tokens over a WebSocket
examples.code:
import asyncio
from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect

app = FastAPI()

async def stream_model(prompt: str):
    # Simulate a model emitting tokens over time
    for word in f"You said: {prompt}".split():
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
        yield word + " "

@app.websocket("/ws/chat")
async def chat(ws: WebSocket):
    await ws.accept()
    try:
        while True:
            prompt = await ws.receive_text()
            async for token in stream_model(prompt):
                await ws.send_text(token)
            await ws.send_text("[DONE]")
    except WebSocketDisconnect:
        print("client disconnected")

examples.title: Browser client consuming the stream

quiz.text: What is the key difference between a WebSocket connection and a normal HTTP request/response?
options: WebSockets can only send text, never binary data
WebSockets keep a persistent, full-duplex connection so both sides can push messages anytime
WebSockets do not require any initial handshake
WebSockets are slower than HTTP for every use case
quiz.correct_answer: WebSockets keep a persistent, full-duplex connection so both sides can push messages anytime
quiz.explanation: After the upgrade handshake, the single connection stays open and both client and server can send messages freely.

quiz.text: Why are WebSockets well suited to LLM chat interfaces?
options: They let the server push each generated token immediately for a live typing effect
They eliminate the need for authentication
quiz.correct_answer: They let the server push each generated token immediately for a live typing effect
quiz.explanation: Streaming tokens as they're produced makes the app feel fast and responsive instead of showing a long spinner.

quiz.text: A simpler, one-way (server-to-client only) alternative to WebSockets for streaming is ______ (SSE).
quiz.explanation: SSE streams from server to client over plain HTTP and is often enough when you don't need client-to-server streaming.

title: MLOps
description:
This phase takes you from "a model that works on my laptop" to "a model that runs reliably in production." You will learn to package training and inference code into reproducible Docker images, orchestrate workloads on Kubernetes, track experiments and register models with MLflow and Weights & Biases, serve predictions behind real APIs, wire up CI/CD so every change is tested and deployed automatically, and monitor live models for latency, errors, and data drift. MLOps is the difference between a research notebook and a system real users depend on, and it is one of the most in-demand skill sets for AI engineers because most models fail in operations, not in training. By the end you will have the vocabulary and hands-on muscle memory to ship models the way senior teams do.
Projects to build: Containerize, track, serve and monitor a model end to end.
Resources: MLflow, Weights & Biases, Kubernetes docs.

lessons.title: Docker for ML
lessons.description: Package model code, dependencies, and system libraries into a single reproducible image that runs identically anywhere.
lessons.content_markdown:
A Docker **image** is a frozen, layered snapshot of a filesystem — your Python version, pip packages, CUDA libraries, model weights, and code — built from a text recipe called a `Dockerfile`. A **container** is a running instance of that image. The value for ML is reproducibility: the classic 'works on my machine' failure disappears because the image *is* the machine. No more mismatched NumPy versions or a missing `libgomp` breaking scikit-learn in staging.

Images are built in layers, one per instruction. Docker caches each layer, so ordering matters: copy and install `requirements.txt` *before* copying your source code. Then editing a `.py` file only invalidates the cheap final layer instead of forcing a full dependency reinstall.

## Key ideas
- **Dockerfile** = build recipe; **image** = built artifact; **container** = a running image.
- **Layer caching**: put slow, rarely-changing steps (dependency install) early; fast, often-changing steps (your code) late.
- Use a **slim or specific base tag** (`python:3.11-slim`) not `latest` — pin versions so builds are deterministic.
- **`.dockerignore`** keeps `.git`, data, and virtualenvs out of the build context so images stay small.
- **Bind mount data at runtime** (`-v`) rather than baking multi-GB datasets into the image.
- For GPU inference use an `nvidia/cuda` base and run with `--gpus all`.

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["uvicorn", "serve:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
```

```bash
docker build -t my-model:0.1 .
docker run --rm -p 8000:8000 my-model:0.1
```

## Why it matters for AI
Every downstream MLOps tool assumes your model is containerized. Kubernetes schedules containers, model servers ship as containers, and CI/CD pipelines build and push image tags. A clean, small, deterministic image is the atomic unit of deployment — get this right and serving, scaling, and rollback all become tractable. Pinning dependencies inside the image also protects you from a silent upstream library change quietly altering your model's numerical outputs.

examples.title: Dockerfile for a FastAPI model server
examples.code:
FROM python:3.11-slim

# System deps some ML libs need (e.g. lightgbm/opencv)
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    libgomp1 && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

WORKDIR /app

# Install deps first so this layer is cached across code edits
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Now copy source (changes here won't reinstall deps)
COPY . .

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["uvicorn", "serve:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]

examples.title: Build, run, and inspect an image
examples.code:
# Build and tag with a version
docker build -t my-model:0.1 .

# Run, mapping container port 8000 to host 8000
docker run --rm -p 8000:8000 my-model:0.1

# Mount a local model directory instead of baking weights in
docker run --rm -p 8000:8000 -v "$(pwd)/weights:/app/weights" my-model:0.1

# See image size and layers
docker images my-model
docker history my-model:0.1

quiz.text: Why should you COPY requirements.txt and run pip install BEFORE copying the rest of your source code?
options: It reduces the final image size by half
Docker layer caching skips reinstalling dependencies when only source code changes
It is required for GPU support
quiz.correct_answer: Docker layer caching skips reinstalling dependencies when only source code changes
quiz.explanation: Each instruction is a cached layer, so installing deps early means editing code only rebuilds the cheap final layers.

quiz.text: A running instance of a Docker image is called a ________.
quiz.explanation: An image is the static artifact; a container is a live, running instance of it.

quiz.text: Which base tag choice makes builds most reproducible?
options: ubuntu with Python compiled at build time
quiz.explanation: Pinning a specific version tag avoids silent changes that 'latest' introduces on rebuild.

lessons.title: Kubernetes Basics
lessons.description: Understand Pods, Deployments, and Services well enough to run and scale a containerized model on a cluster.
lessons.content_markdown:
Kubernetes (K8s) is a system that runs your containers across a fleet of machines and keeps them running. You describe the *desired state* in YAML — 'I want 3 replicas of this image, each reachable on port 8000' — and Kubernetes continuously reconciles reality to match it. If a container crashes, a node dies, or traffic spikes, K8s reschedules and heals without you paging anyone at 3am.

The core objects:
- **Pod**: the smallest deployable unit — one (or a few tightly-coupled) containers sharing a network address. Pods are disposable and get replaced, not repaired.
- **Deployment**: declares *how many* Pod replicas you want and manages rolling updates and rollbacks. This is what you actually write for a stateless model server.
- **Service**: a stable virtual IP and DNS name that load-balances across the Pods behind it, since individual Pod IPs come and go.

## Key ideas
- Kubernetes is **declarative**: you `kubectl apply` a spec and a control loop drives toward it — you don't run imperative start/stop commands.
- **Deployments own ReplicaSets**, which own Pods; scaling changes the replica count and K8s adds/removes Pods.
- **Liveness/readiness probes** let K8s know when to restart a Pod and when it is ready to receive traffic — critical for models with slow warmup.
- **Resource requests/limits** (CPU, memory, `nvidia.com/gpu`) let the scheduler pack workloads and protect neighbors.
- **HorizontalPodAutoscaler** scales replicas on CPU or custom metrics like request latency.

```bash
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl get pods -w
kubectl scale deployment/model --replicas=5
kubectl logs deploy/model
```

## Why it matters for AI
Inference services need to survive node failures, absorb traffic bursts, and roll out new model versions with zero downtime — exactly what Deployments and Services provide. GPU scheduling, autoscaling under load, and canary rollouts of a retrained model are all Kubernetes-native operations. Tools you will meet later — KServe, Seldon, Ray Serve, Kubeflow — are all built directly on these primitives, so Pods/Deployments/Services are the foundation the entire serving stack stands on.

examples.title: Deployment + Service for a model server
examples.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: model
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: model
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: model
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: server
          image: my-model:0.1
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "500m"
              memory: "512Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "1"
              memory: "1Gi"
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: model
spec:
  selector:
    app: model
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8000
  type: ClusterIP

examples.title: Common kubectl workflow
examples.code:
# Apply the desired state
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

# Watch pods come up
kubectl get pods -l app=model -w

# Scale to handle more traffic
kubectl scale deployment/model --replicas=5

# Roll out a new image version (zero-downtime)
kubectl set image deployment/model server=my-model:0.2

# If it misbehaves, roll back
kubectl rollout undo deployment/model

# Tail logs from all matching pods
kubectl logs deploy/model --tail=50

quiz.text: What is the role of a Kubernetes Service?
options: It provides a stable IP/DNS name that load-balances across changing Pods
It defines how many replicas to run
quiz.correct_answer: It provides a stable IP/DNS name that load-balances across changing Pods
quiz.explanation: Pod IPs are ephemeral, so a Service gives clients one durable address that routes to healthy Pods.

quiz.text: The Kubernetes object you use to declare a desired number of stateless replicas and manage rolling updates is a ________.
quiz.explanation: A Deployment manages ReplicaSets and Pods, handling scaling, updates, and rollbacks.

quiz.text: Kubernetes is described as 'declarative' because you:
options: Run start and stop commands for each container manually
Describe the desired state and a control loop reconciles reality to it
SSH into each node to launch containers
Must restart Pods yourself when they crash
quiz.correct_answer: Describe the desired state and a control loop reconciles reality to it
quiz.explanation: You specify what you want; Kubernetes continuously works to make the cluster match that spec.

lessons.title: MLflow
lessons.description: Track experiments, log metrics and artifacts, and register versioned models so training runs are reproducible and comparable.
lessons.content_markdown:
MLflow is an open-source platform for the ML lifecycle. Its most-used piece is **Tracking**: as your training script runs, you log parameters, metrics, and artifacts (the trained model, plots, config) to a tracking server, and every execution becomes a searchable **run** grouped under an **experiment**. Six months later you can answer 'which hyperparameters produced our best F1?' instead of guessing from a folder of `model_final_v2_REAL.pkl` files.

The four components: **Tracking** (log runs), **Projects** (package code with a reproducible entry point), **Models** (a standard flavor-based packaging format), and the **Model Registry** (versioned, stage-managed models: `Staging`, `Production`, `Archived`).

## Key ideas
- **Params vs metrics**: params are inputs you set (learning rate, depth); metrics are measured outcomes (accuracy, loss) and can be logged per-step to plot curves.
- **Artifacts** are files — the serialized model, a confusion matrix PNG, the requirements — stored alongside the run.
- **Autologging** (`mlflow.sklearn.autolog()`, also for PyTorch/XGBoost/LightGBM) captures params, metrics, and the model with almost no code.
- The **Model Registry** gives each model a version number and a stage, enabling promotion and rollback separate from retraining.
- Runs can be **nested** and **tagged** (git commit, dataset hash) for full lineage.

```python
import mlflow

mlflow.set_experiment("churn")
with mlflow.start_run():
    mlflow.log_param("max_depth", 6)
    mlflow.log_metric("f1", 0.87)
    mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, "model")
```

## Why it matters for AI
Without tracking, ML work is irreproducible and un-auditable — a serious problem when a model makes decisions about credit, health, or content. MLflow gives you an experiment ledger, apples-to-apples comparison across runs, and a registry that decouples 'which model version is live' from the training code. That registry becomes the handoff point to your serving and CI/CD stages: your deploy pipeline simply asks for the `Production`-stage model by name and version.

examples.title: Tracking a training run with autolog + registry
examples.code:
import mlflow
import mlflow.sklearn
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score

X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)
Xtr, Xte, ytr, yte = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=0)

mlflow.set_experiment("cancer-clf")
mlflow.sklearn.autolog()  # logs params, metrics, and the model automatically

with mlflow.start_run() as run:
    depth = 6
    clf = RandomForestClassifier(max_depth=depth, random_state=0)
    clf.fit(Xtr, ytr)
    f1 = f1_score(yte, clf.predict(Xte))
    mlflow.log_metric("test_f1", f1)   # extra custom metric

    # Register this run's model into the Model Registry
    mlflow.register_model(
        model_uri=f"runs:/{run.info.run_id}/model",
        name="cancer-clf",
    )
print("logged run:", run.info.run_id, "f1=", f1)

examples.title: Launch the tracking UI and load a registered model
examples.code:
# Start the local MLflow tracking server + UI at http://localhost:5000
mlflow ui --port 5000

# (in Python) load the Production-stage model by name
python -c "import mlflow.pyfunc; m = mlflow.pyfunc.load_model('models:/cancer-clf/Production'); print(m)"

quiz.text: In MLflow, what is the difference between a param and a metric?
options: Params are outputs, metrics are inputs
Params are inputs you set (e.g. learning rate); metrics are measured outcomes (e.g. accuracy)
Params are numbers, metrics are always strings
quiz.correct_answer: Params are inputs you set (e.g. learning rate); metrics are measured outcomes (e.g. accuracy)
quiz.explanation: Params describe how the run was configured; metrics record what the run achieved and can be logged over steps.

quiz.text: The MLflow component that gives models version numbers and stages like Staging and Production is the Model ________.
quiz.explanation: The Model Registry manages versioned models and their lifecycle stages, enabling promotion and rollback.

quiz.text: What does mlflow.sklearn.autolog() do?
options: Deploys the model to production automatically
Automatically logs params, metrics, and the trained model with minimal extra code
quiz.correct_answer: Automatically logs params, metrics, and the trained model with minimal extra code
quiz.explanation: Autolog hooks into the framework so you don't have to manually log each param and metric.

lessons.title: Weights & Biases
lessons.description: Use W&B to log rich experiments, visualize training in real time, sweep hyperparameters, and version datasets and models.
lessons.content_markdown:
Weights & Biases (W&B, or `wandb`) is a hosted experiment-tracking and collaboration platform. Like MLflow it logs params, metrics, and artifacts, but it leans into **real-time, richly-visual dashboards**: you watch loss curves stream in live from a browser while training runs, compare dozens of runs on interactive parallel-coordinate plots, and log images, audio, tables, and even model predictions for inspection. It shines for deep learning and for teams that want a shared, always-on view.

A typical flow: call `wandb.init()` to start a run (grouped by **project**), then `wandb.log({...})` each step. W&B also provides **Sweeps** (automated hyperparameter search via grid, random, or Bayesian strategies) and **Artifacts** (versioned, deduplicated datasets and models with full lineage graphs).

## Key ideas
- **`wandb.init(project=...)`** starts a run; **`wandb.log(dict)`** streams metrics that render as live charts.
- **`wandb.config`** stores hyperparameters so runs are comparable and reproducible.
- **Sweeps**: define a search space and strategy in YAML; an **agent** launches runs and W&B ranks them automatically.
- **Artifacts** version data/models and track which run produced or consumed each — reproducibility and lineage.
- Log **media** (`wandb.Image`, `wandb.Table`) to debug model behavior, not just scalar metrics.
- W&B vs MLflow: W&B is hosted/collaborative and visualization-first; MLflow is open-source and often self-hosted. Many teams use both.

```python
import wandb
wandb.init(project="demo", config={"lr": 3e-4})
for step in range(100):
    wandb.log({"loss": 1/(step+1)})
wandb.finish()
```

## Why it matters for AI
Deep learning runs are long and expensive, and small config differences produce big outcome swings. Live dashboards let you kill a diverging run early instead of wasting GPU-hours, Sweeps automate the tedious hyperparameter search that dominates model quality, and Artifacts give you the dataset/model lineage that auditors and teammates need. When you are training LLMs or vision models across a team, this shared, searchable record of every experiment is what keeps the work reproducible and honest.

examples.title: Logging a PyTorch-style training loop
examples.code:
import wandb
import random

wandb.init(project="mnist-demo", config={"lr": 3e-4, "epochs": 5, "batch": 64})
cfg = wandb.config

for epoch in range(cfg.epochs):
    # ... your real train step here; we simulate metrics ...
    train_loss = 1.0 / (epoch + 1) + random.uniform(0, 0.05)
    val_acc = 0.7 + 0.05 * epoch
    wandb.log({"epoch": epoch, "train_loss": train_loss, "val_acc": val_acc})

# Version the trained weights as an artifact
art = wandb.Artifact("model", type="model")
art.add_file("model.pt")  # assumes you saved weights to model.pt
wandb.log_artifact(art)
wandb.finish()

examples.title: Hyperparameter sweep config + agent
examples.code:
# sweep.yaml
program: train.py
method: bayes           # grid | random | bayes
metric:
  name: val_acc
  goal: maximize
parameters:
  lr:
    distribution: log_uniform_values
    min: 0.00001
    max: 0.01
  batch:
    values: [32, 64, 128]
# Run it:
#   wandb sweep sweep.yaml      -> prints a SWEEP_ID
#   wandb agent <SWEEP_ID>       -> launches runs that explore the space

quiz.text: In W&B, the feature that automates hyperparameter search using grid, random, or Bayesian strategies is called a ________.
quiz.explanation: Sweeps define a search space and let W&B agents launch and rank runs automatically.

quiz.text: Which call streams a metric so it renders as a live chart during training?
quiz.explanation: wandb.log sends step-wise metrics that the dashboard plots in real time.

quiz.text: What are W&B Artifacts primarily used for?
options: Versioning datasets and models with lineage tracking
quiz.correct_answer: Versioning datasets and models with lineage tracking
quiz.explanation: Artifacts store versioned, deduplicated data/models and record which runs produced or consumed them.

lessons.title: Model Serving
lessons.description: Expose a trained model as a low-latency API and understand batching, warmup, and online vs batch inference.
lessons.content_markdown:
Model serving turns a saved model into a service that answers prediction requests. The dominant pattern is **online (real-time) serving**: an HTTP/gRPC endpoint loads the model once at startup and returns predictions per request in milliseconds. The contrast is **batch/offline inference**, where you score a large dataset on a schedule and write results to a store. Choose online when a user waits for the answer (fraud check, recommendation) and batch when you can precompute (nightly churn scores).

A minimal server loads weights at import time (never per request — loading is the expensive part), validates input, runs inference, and returns JSON. Beyond the basics you add a **`/health` endpoint** for Kubernetes probes, **warmup** (a dummy inference at startup so the first real request isn't slow), and **dynamic batching** (grouping concurrent requests into one GPU forward pass for throughput). Production teams often reach for purpose-built servers — **TorchServe, TensorFlow Serving, NVIDIA Triton, KServe, Ray Serve, vLLM** for LLMs — which give batching, multi-model hosting, and metrics out of the box.

## Key ideas
- **Load the model once at startup**, reuse across requests; loading per request kills latency.
- **Online vs batch**: latency-sensitive single predictions vs high-throughput bulk scoring.
- **Health/readiness endpoint** so orchestrators know when the Pod can take traffic.
- **Dynamic batching** trades a few ms of latency for large throughput gains on GPUs.
- **Validate and version the request/response schema** (Pydantic) — inputs from the wild are messy.
- Track **p50/p95/p99 latency**, not just averages; tail latency is what users feel.

## Why it matters for AI
A model delivers zero value until something can call it. Serving is where accuracy meets the real-world constraints of latency budgets, concurrency, and cost-per-request. Getting the serving layer right — fast startup, proper batching, health checks, schema validation — is what lets your Kubernetes Deployment scale cleanly and your monitoring capture meaningful signals. It is the direct bridge from the model registry to a product feature users actually touch.

examples.title: FastAPI online inference server with validation and health check
Call the endpoint
examples.code:
# Start it
uvicorn serve:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

# Health check (used by Kubernetes readiness probe)
curl http://localhost:8000/health

# Send a prediction request
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/predict \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"values": [5.1, 3.5, 1.4, 0.2]}'

quiz.text: Why should a serving app load the model at startup rather than inside the request handler?
options: Model loading is expensive, so doing it once avoids adding that cost to every request's latency
FastAPI forbids loading models in handlers
quiz.correct_answer: Model loading is expensive, so doing it once avoids adding that cost to every request's latency
quiz.explanation: Deserializing weights is slow; loading once at startup keeps per-request latency low.

quiz.text: Grouping several concurrent requests into a single forward pass to improve GPU throughput is called dynamic ________.
quiz.explanation: Dynamic batching trades a little latency for much higher throughput on accelerators.

quiz.text: Which scenario is best served by batch (offline) inference rather than online serving?
options: A real-time fraud check while a payment is processing
Autocomplete suggestions as a user types
Precomputing nightly churn scores for all customers
A chatbot responding to a live message
quiz.correct_answer: Precomputing nightly churn scores for all customers
quiz.explanation: When results can be computed ahead of time on a schedule, batch inference is cheaper and simpler than a live endpoint.

lessons.title: CI/CD
lessons.description: Automate testing, image builds, and deployment so every change to model code ships safely and repeatably.
lessons.content_markdown:
**CI/CD** stands for Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery/Deployment. **CI** runs automatically on every push: install deps, lint, run unit tests, and — for ML — validate data and check that the model meets a minimum quality bar. **CD** takes a passing build and ships it: build and push the Docker image, then update the Kubernetes Deployment. A pipeline is defined as code (e.g. a GitHub Actions YAML in `.github/workflows/`) so the process is versioned, reviewable, and identical for everyone.

ML adds wrinkles beyond normal software CI/CD. You often gate deployment on a **model-quality test** ('new model's F1 must be ≥ the current Production model'), you pull the model from the **registry** rather than committing weights to git, and you frequently deploy with **canary or blue-green** strategies so a bad model reaches only a slice of traffic first. The goal is that shipping a retrained model is a routine, low-drama, reversible event — not a hand-cranked ritual.

## Key ideas
- **CI**: on every push, run lint + unit tests + data/model validation; fail fast on regressions.
- **CD**: build/push a versioned image, then roll it out (`kubectl set image` or GitOps like Argo CD).
- **Pipeline as code**: the workflow lives in the repo, so it's reviewed and reproducible.
- **Quality gates**: block deploy unless the candidate model beats a metric threshold.
- **Progressive delivery**: canary/blue-green rollouts plus automatic rollback on error-rate spikes.
- **Secrets** (registry creds, cluster tokens) come from the CI system's secret store, never the repo.

## Why it matters for AI
Models change far more often than traditional code — new data arrives, you retrain weekly, hyperparameters drift. Manual deploys don't scale and invite human error at exactly the moment (a fresh model on live users) when mistakes are costly. Automated pipelines with quality gates and one-click rollback turn 'deploy a new model' from a risky event into a repeatable, auditable, reversible operation, and they close the loop with your MLflow registry, Docker images, and Kubernetes Deployments from earlier lessons.

examples.title: GitHub Actions: test, build image, deploy to Kubernetes
examples.code:
name: ci-cd
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.11" }
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pytest -q                      # unit tests
      - run: python validate_model.py       # gate: F1 >= threshold

  build-and-deploy:
    needs: test                              # only runs if tests pass
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Build & push image
        run: |
          echo "${{ secrets.REGISTRY_TOKEN }}" | docker login ghcr.io -u ci --password-stdin
          docker build -t ghcr.io/acme/model:${{ github.sha }} .
          docker push ghcr.io/acme/model:${{ github.sha }}
      - name: Roll out to cluster
        run: kubectl set image deployment/model server=ghcr.io/acme/model:${{ github.sha }}

examples.title: A model-quality gate that fails the build
examples.code:
# validate_model.py -- exit non-zero to block deployment
import sys, json
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score
import joblib, numpy as np

X_val = np.load("data/X_val.npy")
y_val = np.load("data/y_val.npy")
model = joblib.load("model.joblib")

f1 = f1_score(y_val, model.predict(X_val))
THRESHOLD = 0.85
print(json.dumps({"f1": round(f1, 4), "threshold": THRESHOLD}))

if f1 < THRESHOLD:
    print(f"FAIL: f1 {f1:.3f} < {THRESHOLD}")
    sys.exit(1)   # non-zero exit fails the CI job and blocks CD
print("PASS: model meets quality bar")

quiz.text: What primarily distinguishes Continuous Integration (CI) from Continuous Delivery (CD)?
options: CI deploys to production; CD runs tests
CI runs tests/validation on each change; CD builds and ships the passing artifact
They are two names for the same step
CI is manual; CD is also manual
quiz.correct_answer: CI runs tests/validation on each change; CD builds and ships the passing artifact
quiz.explanation: CI verifies correctness on every push; CD automates packaging and deployment of builds that pass.

quiz.text: A CI step that blocks deployment unless a candidate model beats a metric threshold is called a quality ________.
quiz.explanation: Quality gates prevent worse models from reaching production by requiring they clear a metric bar.

quiz.text: Where should registry credentials and cluster tokens used by a pipeline be stored?
options: Committed to the repository in a config file
In the CI system's encrypted secret store, referenced at runtime
quiz.correct_answer: In the CI system's encrypted secret store, referenced at runtime
quiz.explanation: Secrets belong in the CI secret store and are injected at runtime, never committed to the repo or baked into images.

lessons.title: Monitoring
lessons.description: Track a live model's operational health and prediction quality, and detect data and concept drift before users do.
lessons.content_markdown:
Monitoring is how you know a deployed model is still healthy. It splits into two layers. **Operational monitoring** covers the service like any web app: request rate, error rate, and latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99), plus CPU/GPU/memory — the classic signals, often summarized as latency, traffic, errors, saturation. **ML-specific monitoring** covers whether the *predictions* are still trustworthy: input **data drift** (the live feature distribution has moved away from training), **concept drift** (the input→output relationship itself changed), prediction distribution shifts, and — when labels eventually arrive — live accuracy.

The hard part of ML monitoring is that a model can serve fast, error-free responses that are quietly, increasingly wrong because the world changed. Ground-truth labels often arrive late (a loan default shows up months later), so you rely on **proxy signals**: distribution distances like Population Stability Index (PSI) or KS-statistic between a training reference and a live window, and alerts when they cross a threshold. The standard stack is **Prometheus** (scrapes metrics your app exposes) plus **Grafana** (dashboards and alerts); ML-focused tools like **Evidently** or **NannyML** compute drift and data-quality reports.

## Key ideas
- **Operational**: latency p95/p99, error rate, throughput, resource saturation — the SRE 'golden signals'.
- **Data drift**: live feature distribution diverges from the training reference (measure with PSI, KS, or population comparisons).
- **Concept drift**: the target relationship changes, so accuracy degrades even if inputs look similar.
- **Delayed labels**: monitor proxy signals now; reconcile true accuracy when ground truth lands.
- **Log predictions + inputs** (sampled) so you can debug, compute drift, and build the next training set.
- **Alert with thresholds and hysteresis** to page on real regressions, not noise; feed severe drift back into a retraining trigger.

## Why it matters for AI
Models decay — this is called staleness, and unlike bugs it happens silently with zero code changes. A recommender trained pre-holiday misreads December; a fraud model misses a brand-new attack pattern. Monitoring is the feedback loop that catches decay early, quantifies it, and triggers retraining, closing the MLOps lifecycle: you containerized, orchestrated, tracked, served, and shipped a model — monitoring is what keeps it *good* in the world it now lives in.

examples.title: Expose Prometheus metrics from a model server
Detect feature drift with a PSI calculation
examples.code:
import numpy as np

def population_stability_index(ref, live, bins=10):
    # Bin edges from the training reference distribution
    edges = np.quantile(ref, np.linspace(0, 1, bins + 1))
    edges[0], edges[-1] = -np.inf, np.inf
    ref_pct = np.histogram(ref, edges)[0] / len(ref)
    live_pct = np.histogram(live, edges)[0] / len(live)
    # Avoid divide-by-zero
    ref_pct = np.clip(ref_pct, 1e-6, None)
    live_pct = np.clip(live_pct, 1e-6, None)
    return np.sum((live_pct - ref_pct) * np.log(live_pct / ref_pct))

train = np.random.normal(0, 1, 10000)
live_ok = np.random.normal(0, 1, 2000)
live_drift = np.random.normal(1.2, 1, 2000)   # shifted distribution

print("PSI stable :", round(population_stability_index(train, live_ok), 3))
print("PSI drifted:", round(population_stability_index(train, live_drift), 3))
# Rule of thumb: <0.1 no shift, 0.1-0.25 moderate, >0.25 significant drift

quiz.text: A model returns fast, error-free responses but its predictions are increasingly wrong because real-world inputs have shifted away from the training data. This is best described as:
quiz.explanation: Data drift is when the live input distribution diverges from the training distribution, degrading predictions even though the service is operationally fine.

quiz.text: The common open-source pairing is ________ for scraping metrics and Grafana for dashboards and alerts.
quiz.explanation: Prometheus scrapes and stores time-series metrics that Grafana then visualizes and alerts on.

quiz.text: Why must ML monitoring often rely on proxy signals like distribution distance instead of live accuracy?
options: Ground-truth labels frequently arrive with a long delay
Proxy signals are always more accurate than labels
quiz.correct_answer: Ground-truth labels frequently arrive with a long delay
quiz.explanation: Because true outcomes can lag by weeks or months, drift metrics act as early proxies until labels arrive to confirm accuracy.

title: AI Agents
description:
This phase turns a language model from a text generator into an autonomous system that can act on the world. You will learn how agents invoke external tools through structured function calling, how multiple specialized agents divide labor and communicate, how agents persist and retrieve memory across turns and sessions, how they decompose goals into executable plans, and how they drive real web browsers to complete tasks no API exposes. These are the exact capabilities behind coding copilots, research assistants, and automated operations bots, so mastering them is the difference between an ML practitioner who trains models and an AI engineer who ships products. By the end you will understand the control loops, failure modes, and orchestration patterns that production agent frameworks are built on.
Projects to build: Coding Agent, Research Agent, Travel Planner, Recruitment Agent.
Resources: LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen docs.

lessons.title: Tool Calling
lessons.description: How an LLM requests structured calls to external functions and incorporates their results into a reasoning loop.
lessons.content_markdown:
Tool calling (also called function calling) is the mechanism that lets a language model *do* things instead of only *describe* things. You give the model a machine-readable schema of functions it may use — each with a name, a natural-language description, and a JSON-Schema for its arguments. When the model decides a function is needed, it does not run it; it emits a structured JSON payload naming the tool and its arguments. **Your code executes the function** and feeds the result back into the conversation. The model then continues, possibly calling more tools, until it produces a final answer.

The critical insight for beginners: the LLM never touches your database or filesystem directly. It only *proposes* calls. This client-side execution boundary is what makes tools safe(r) and testable — you validate and sandbox every call.

## Key ideas

- **The agent loop**: `prompt -> model -> (tool_call?) -> execute -> feed result back -> repeat -> final answer`. This while-loop is the heart of every agent.
- **Schemas are the interface**: The model chooses tools based almost entirely on the `name` and `description` fields. Vague descriptions cause wrong tool selection — write them like API docs.
- **Structured, not free-text**: Arguments come back as validated JSON, so you avoid brittle regex parsing of prose.
- **Multi-step**: A single user request can trigger several tool calls in sequence (search, then read, then compute).
- **Errors are inputs**: When a tool raises, you return the error text as the tool result. A good model reads it and self-corrects.

```python
tools = [{
  "name": "get_weather",
  "description": "Get current temperature in Celsius for a city.",
  "input_schema": {
    "type": "object",
    "properties": {"city": {"type": "string"}},
    "required": ["city"],
  },
}]
```

## Why it matters for AI

Tool calling is the foundation every other agent capability builds on: memory is a `save`/`recall` tool, planning is often a `write_plan` tool, and multi-agent systems expose whole agents as callable tools. Retrieval-augmented generation, code execution, and API automation are all just tools. If you understand the propose-execute-feedback loop deeply, you can reason about — and debug — any agent framework, because underneath LangGraph, CrewAI, and AutoGen, this exact loop is running.

examples.title: A minimal tool-calling loop with the Anthropic SDK
Deriving a JSON schema automatically from a Python function

quiz.text: When an LLM issues a tool call, what actually happens?
options: The model runs the function itself inside its weights
The model emits structured JSON naming the tool and arguments, and your code executes it
The API provider executes the function on their servers automatically
The tool result is generated by the model as plausible text
quiz.correct_answer: The model emits structured JSON naming the tool and arguments, and your code executes it
quiz.explanation: The model only proposes calls; execution happens client-side, which is what keeps tools testable and sandboxable.

quiz.text: A model keeps picking the wrong tool. What is the most likely fix?
options: Rewrite each tool's name and description to be clearer and more specific
Add more example conversations to the system prompt only
quiz.correct_answer: Rewrite each tool's name and description to be clearer and more specific
quiz.explanation: Tool selection is driven almost entirely by the name and description fields, so treating them like precise API docs matters most.

quiz.text: The core control structure of an agent is a ____ that repeats: call the model, run any requested tools, feed results back, until no tool is requested.
quiz.explanation: The agent loop (a while-loop) repeatedly executes proposed tools and returns their results until the model produces a final answer.

lessons.title: Multi-Agent Systems
lessons.description: How to decompose complex work across several specialized agents that communicate and coordinate.
lessons.content_markdown:
A multi-agent system splits a hard problem across several LLM-powered agents, each with its own role, system prompt, and tool set, that pass messages to one another. Instead of one giant prompt trying to be researcher, coder, and critic simultaneously, you give each concern its own agent. This mirrors how a human team works: a manager delegates, specialists execute, and a reviewer checks.

The motivation is both quality and control. A single agent handling many responsibilities suffers from *context dilution* — its instructions compete, and long histories degrade focus. Separate agents keep each context small and purpose-built, and they let you swap models per role (a cheap model for extraction, a strong model for synthesis).

## Key ideas

- **Common topologies**:
  - *Supervisor / orchestrator*: one router agent decides which specialist to invoke next (LangGraph's default pattern).
  - *Sequential pipeline*: agents run in a fixed order, output feeding input (CrewAI "crew" with tasks).
  - *Group chat / debate*: agents talk in a shared thread until a stop condition (AutoGen's `GroupChat`).
- **Handoff mechanics**: One agent's final message becomes another agent's input. Frameworks model this as either a shared message list or explicit `transfer_to(agent)` tools.
- **Roles reduce prompt bloat**: Each agent's system prompt only describes *its* job, keeping instructions crisp.
- **Termination is a design problem**: Without a clear stop condition (max turns, a `finish` tool, or a supervisor verdict), agents loop forever and burn tokens.
- **Cost multiplies**: N agents chatting means N model calls per round — parallelize independent work, serialize dependent work.

## Why it matters for AI

The hardest real-world tasks — writing and reviewing a codebase, researching a topic from many sources, screening candidates against a rubric — are naturally decomposable. Multi-agent design is how you make LLM systems *reliable* on these: a critic agent catches the generator's mistakes, and a supervisor enforces structure that a single free-running agent lacks. Every framework in this phase (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen) is fundamentally a library for wiring agents together and routing messages between them, so understanding topologies and termination lets you pick and debug any of them.

examples.title: A supervisor routing between two specialist agents (plain Python)
A sequential two-agent crew with CrewAI
examples.code:
from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process

researcher = Agent(
    role="Senior Researcher",
    goal="Find 3 concrete facts about {topic}",
    backstory="A meticulous analyst who cites specifics.",
    verbose=True,
)
writer = Agent(
    role="Technical Writer",
    goal="Write a tight 4-sentence summary from the research",
    backstory="An editor who values clarity over fluff.",
)

research_task = Task(
    description="Research key facts about {topic}.",
    expected_output="3 bullet points with specifics.",
    agent=researcher,
)
write_task = Task(
    description="Summarize the research into 4 sentences.",
    expected_output="A polished paragraph.",
    agent=writer,
    context=[research_task],  # handoff: gets research_task's output
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, writer],
    tasks=[research_task, write_task],
    process=Process.sequential,
)
print(crew.kickoff(inputs={"topic": "vector databases"}))

quiz.text: Why split a task across multiple specialized agents instead of one large agent?
options: It always uses fewer tokens than a single agent
It keeps each agent's context small and focused, reducing instruction conflicts and context dilution
It removes the need for tool calling
quiz.correct_answer: It keeps each agent's context small and focused, reducing instruction conflicts and context dilution
quiz.explanation: Separate agents each carry a small, purpose-built context, avoiding the competing instructions a single overloaded prompt suffers from.

quiz.text: In a supervisor/orchestrator topology, the supervisor's main job is to:
options: Decide which specialist agent to invoke next and route messages
Store long-term memory for the system
quiz.correct_answer: Decide which specialist agent to invoke next and route messages
quiz.explanation: A supervisor acts as a router, delegating to specialists rather than doing the specialized work directly.

quiz.text: Without a clear ____ condition (like max turns or a finish tool), agents in a group chat can loop forever and waste tokens.
quiz.explanation: Defining a termination/stop condition is essential to prevent multi-agent conversations from running indefinitely.

lessons.title: Memory
lessons.description: How agents store and retrieve information within a conversation and across sessions to overcome the fixed context window.
lessons.content_markdown:
An LLM is stateless: each API call only knows what you put in its context window. **Memory** is the machinery you build around the model to give it continuity — recalling earlier turns, facts about the user, and knowledge from past sessions that no longer fit (or never fit) in the prompt. Without it, an agent forgets your name the moment the conversation scrolls past the window.

There are two distinct kinds. **Short-term (working) memory** is the running message list for the current session — bounded by the context window, so long chats must be trimmed or summarized. **Long-term memory** is external storage (a database or vector store) that persists across sessions and is queried on demand, so its size is effectively unlimited.

## Key ideas

- **Short-term strategies**: keep the last N turns (windowing), or periodically *summarize* older turns into a compact note that replaces them (summary buffer).
- **Long-term via embeddings**: store memories as vectors; at each turn, embed the user's message and retrieve the top-k most similar memories to inject into the prompt. This is the same retrieval used in RAG.
- **Memory types worth separating**: *semantic* (facts: "user is vegetarian"), *episodic* (events: "last week they booked Tokyo"), *procedural* (learned instructions/preferences).
- **Writing is a decision**: agents shouldn't store everything. A `save_memory` tool lets the model choose what's durable; a summarizer can extract facts automatically.
- **Retrieval is ranking**: relevance beats recency — semantic search surfaces the *right* memory even from months ago.

## Why it matters for AI

Memory is what makes an assistant feel like it *knows you* and lets a research or coding agent work across a long task without re-explaining context every message.
